> 5. **Median formula for even $n$:** Many students incorrectly pick a single middle value. Always average the two central values.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Descriptive analytics answers **"What happened?"** — not "Why?" or "What next?"
- **Mean** is sensitive to outliers; **median** is robust.
- **Standard deviation** is the square root of variance and shares units with the data.
- **Variance uses $\dfrac{1}{n-1}$** for samples and $\dfrac{1}{N}$ for populations.
- **Skewness** quantifies asymmetry: positive (right), negative (left), zero (symmetric).
- **Kurtosis** quantifies peak sharpness and tail heaviness.
- **Z-score standardization** transforms data to mean 0 and standard deviation 1.
- **Coefficient of Variation** allows dimensionless comparison of dispersion across datasets.
- **Box plots** are excellent for visualizing the five-number summary and detecting outliers.
- Always check the **data type** (categorical vs. numerical) before selecting a chart.
- **IQR-based outlier rule:** Any value $< Q\_1 - 1.5 \cdot IQR$ or $> Q\_3 + 1.5 \cdot IQR$ is an outlier.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mining Frequent Item Sets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT751 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mining Frequent Item Sets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Frequent Itemset Mining (FIM)** is a fundamental data mining technique that aims to discover sets of items (itemsets) that co-occur frequently together in a transactional database, subject to a user-specified minimum support threshold. It forms the foundational step for **Market Basket Analysis**, **Association Rule Mining**, and various downstream pattern-discovery tasks in retail, bioinformatics, and recommendation systems.

Formally, given:
- A universe of items $I = \{i_1, i_2, \ldots, i_m\}$
- A transactional database $D = \{T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n\}$ where each $T_k \subseteq I$
- A **minimum support threshold** $\sigma$ (or $min\_sup$)

An itemset $X \subseteq I$ is termed **frequent** if its **support count** $\sigma(X)$ meets or exceeds $\sigma$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU module specifically expects mastery of (1) the Apriori Algorithm, (2) the generation and pruning of candidate itemsets ($C_k$ and $L_k$), (3) the Downward Closure Property (Apriori Property), and (4) Confidence-based rule generation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are the owner of a supermarket in Kerala observing customers' baskets at the billing counter. You notice that customers who buy **tea** *very often* also buy **sugar** and **milk powder**. Naturally, you would want to:
1. Place these items close together on the shelf.
2. Offer combo discounts.
3. Recommend these items together in your app.

**Frequent Itemset Mining** is the *automated, algorithmic way* of finding such co-purchasing patterns from millions of transactions — without you having to manually observe baskets. The word **"frequent"** is the crux — we only care about combinations that appear *often enough* to be statistically meaningful, not one-off coincidences.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** Frequent itemsets are the "ingredients" — Association Rules are the "recipes" derived from those ingredients. You cannot cook a recipe without knowing the ingredients first.

### 1.3 Key Metrics at a Glance

| Metric | Meaning | Threshold Name |
|---|---|---|
| **Support** | How frequently an itemset appears in the database | $\sigma \geq min\_sup$ |
| **Confidence** | Strength of implication $X \Rightarrow Y$ | $\geq min\_conf$ |
| **Lift** | Correlation between $X$ and $Y$ | $> 1$ = positively correlated |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Downward Closure Property (Apriori Property)

The **Apriori Property** is the single most important theoretical underpinning of frequent itemset mining. It states:

> *If an itemset $X$ is frequent, then **every non-empty subset** of $X$ must also be frequent.*

Conversely, if any subset of $X$ is infrequent, then $X$ itself **must be infrequent** — this is called the **anti-monotone** property, and it allows aggressive **pruning** of the candidate search space.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Every algorithm question you solve relies on this property. Examiners specifically test whether students use it to *avoid generating* impossible candidates.

### 2.2 The Apriori Algorithm — Operational Logic

The Apriori algorithm, proposed by **Agrawal and Srikant (1994)**, operates in two alternating phases:

**Phase 1 — Candidate Generation & Pruning:**
1. Scan the database $D$ to compute support of each 1-itemset $\rightarrow$ obtain $L_1$.
2. Use $L_{k-1}$ to generate candidates $C_k$ via the **join step** ($L_{k-1} \bowtie L_{k-1}$) followed by the **prune step** (delete any candidate whose $(k-1)$-subset is not in $L_{k-1}$).
3. Scan $D$ again to count supports and retain only itemsets meeting $min\_sup$ to form $L_k$.
4. Repeat until $L_k$ becomes empty.

**Phase 2 — Rule Generation (Post-Processing):**
5. For each frequent itemset $l \in L_k$, generate all non-empty proper subsets $s \subset l$.
6. For each subset $s$, output the rule $s \Rightarrow (l - s)$ if its confidence exceeds $min\_conf$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Metric | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Support of itemset $X$ | $\text{sup}(X) = \dfrac{\sigma(X)}{\vert D \vert}$ | Fraction of transactions containing $X$ |
| Support Count | $\sigma(X)$ | Raw number of transactions containing $X$ |
| Confidence of rule $X \Rightarrow Y$ | $\text{conf}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{\text{sup}(X \cup Y)}{\text{sup}(X)}$ | Conditional probability interpretation |
| Lift of rule $X \Rightarrow Y$ | $\text{lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{\text{conf}(X \Rightarrow Y)}{\text{sup}(Y)} = \dfrac{\text{sup}(X \cup Y)}{\text{sup}(X) \cdot \text{sup}(Y)}$ | $> 1$ positive correlation, $= 1$ independent, $< 1$ negative |
| Conviction | $\text{conv}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{1 - \text{sup}(Y)}{1 - \text{conf}(X \Rightarrow Y)}$ | Measures implication direction |
| Apriori Anti-Monotone | $X \text{ frequent} \Rightarrow \forall Y \subseteq X,\ Y \text{ frequent}$ | Used in pruning |
| Candidate Generation (Join) | $C_k = L_{k-1} \bowtie L_{k-1}$ on first $(k-2)$ items | Self-join of $L_{k-1}$ |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

Frequent itemset mining underpins:
- **Retail Analytics:** Shelf optimization, cross-selling, dynamic pricing.
- **Bioinformatics:** Identifying co-expressed gene clusters, motif discovery.
- **Telecommunications:** Analyzing call-detail-record (CDR) patterns for fraud detection.
- **Intrusion Detection Systems:** Mining frequent system-call sequences.
- **Recommendation Engines:** Amazon, Flipkart, Netflix leverage FIM-derived rules.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Complete Apriori Execution

**Given Transaction Database $D$:**

| TID | Items Purchased |
|---|---|
| T1 | Bread, Butter, Milk |
| T2 | Bread, Butter |
| T3 | Butter, Milk |
| T4 | Bread, Milk |
| T5 | Bread, Butter, Milk |

**Parameters:** $min\_sup = 3$ (i.e., **60%** of 5 transactions), $min\_conf = 60\%$.

---

#### Step 1 — Generate $C_1$ (Candidate 1-Itemsets) and $L_1$

Scan $D$ and count occurrences of each individual item:

| Itemset | Support Count $\sigma$ | Support $\sigma / \vert D \vert$ | Frequent? |
|---|---|---|---|
| {Bread} | 4 | 4/5 = 0.80 | ✅ |
| {Butter} | 4 | 4/5 = 0.80 | ✅ |
| {Milk} | 4 | 4/5 = 0.80 | ✅ |

$$
L_1 = \{\{\text{Bread}\},\ \{\text{Butter}\},\ \{\text{Milk}\}\}
$$

---

#### Step 2 — Generate $C_2$ via Self-Join of $L_1$

$$
C_2 = L_1 \bowtie L_1 = \{\{\text{Bread, Butter}\},\ \{\text{Bread, Milk}\},\ \{\text{Butter, Milk}\}\}
$$

Scan $D$ again:

| Itemset | Support Count | Frequent? |
|---|---|---|
| {Bread, Butter} | 3 (T1, T2, T5) | ✅ |
| {Bread, Milk} | 3 (T1, T4, T5) | ✅ |
| {Butter, Milk} | 3 (T1, T3, T5) | ✅ |

$$
L_2 = \{\{\text{Bread, Butter}\},\ \{\text{Bread, Milk}\},\ \{\text{Butter, Milk}\}\}
$$

---

#### Step 3 — Generate $C_3$ and Apply Apriori Pruning

Self-join $L_2$ on first $(3-2)=1$ item:

$$
C_3 = \{\{\text{Bread, Butter, Milk}\}\}
$$

**Apply Pruning (Apriori Property):** Subsets of size 2 of {Bread, Butter, Milk} are {Bread, Butter}, {Bread, Milk}, {Butter, Milk} — all three are in $L_2$. Hence, **candidate survives pruning**.

Scan $D$:

| Itemset | Support Count | Frequent? |
|---|---|---|
| {Bread, Butter, Milk} | 2 (T1, T5) | ❌ (below min_sup = 3) |

$$
L_3 = \emptyset
$$

**Algorithm terminates.** Final frequent itemsets: $L = L_1 \cup L_2$.

---

### 3.2 Association Rule Generation from $L_2$

For each frequent 2-itemset, derive two candidate rules. Confidence formula:

$$
\text{conf}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{\text{sup}(X \cup Y)}{\text{sup}(X)}
$$

| Rule | Computation | Confidence | Strong? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bread $\Rightarrow$ Butter | $\frac{3}{4} = 0.75$ | 75% | ✅ |
| Butter $\Rightarrow$ Bread | $\frac{3}{4} = 0.75$ | 75% | ✅ |
| Bread $\Rightarrow$ Milk | $\frac{3}{4} = 0.75$ | 75% | ✅ |
| Milk $\Rightarrow$ Bread | $\frac{3}{4} = 0.75$ | 75% | ✅ |
| Butter $\Rightarrow$ Milk | $\frac{3}{4} = 0.75$ | 75% | ✅ |
| Milk $\Rightarrow$ Butter | $\frac{3}{4} = 0.75$ | 75% | ✅ |

All six rules are **strong** at $min\_conf = 60\%$.

---

### 3.3 Lift Calculation (Derivation)

For rule **Bread $\Rightarrow$ Milk**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{lift}(\text{Bread} \Rightarrow \text{Milk}) &= \frac{\text{sup}(\text{Bread} \cup \text{Milk})}{\text{sup}(\text{Bread}) \cdot \text{sup}(\text{Milk})} \\
&= \frac{3/5}{(4/5) \cdot (4/5)} \\
&= \frac{0.60}{0.64} \\
&= 0.9375
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Since $\text{lift} < 1$, Bread and Milk are **slightly negatively correlated** — but only marginally. Both still appear together frequently, hence the rule is strong.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strong rule ≠ Positive correlation.** Confidence alone does not imply dependence. Lift corrects for the baseline popularity of the consequent. KTU examiners *frequently* test this distinction.

---

### 3.4 Complete Frequent Itemset Mining Process Flow

The end-to-end pipeline:

$$
\text{Raw Data} \rightarrow \text{Transaction Encoding} \rightarrow \text{Generate } C_1 \rightarrow \text{Prune to } L_1 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow L_k = \emptyset \rightarrow \text{Generate Strong Rules}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Apriori Algorithm — Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START]) --> initD[Load Transaction Database D]
    initD --> setParams[Set min_sup and min_conf thresholds]
    setParams --> genC1[Generate Candidate C1 from single items]
    genC1 --> scanD1[Scan D to compute support counts]
    scanD1 --> prune1{Prune C1 to L1 using min_sup}
    prune1 --> checkL1{L1 is empty?}
    checkL1 -- YES --> stopA([STOP - No frequent itemsets])
    checkL1 -- NO --> genCk[Generate Ck from Lk-1 via self join]
    genCk --> applyApriori[Apply Apriori Property Pruning]
    applyApriori --> scanDk[Scan D to count support for Ck]
    scanDk --> prunek{Prune Ck to Lk using min_sup}
    prunek --> checkLk{Lk is empty?}
    checkLk -- YES --> ruleGen[Generate Association Rules from all Lk]
    checkLk -- NO --> genCk
    ruleGen --> ruleFilter{Confidence greater than or equal min_conf?}
    ruleFilter -- YES --> outputStrong[Output Strong Rule]
    ruleFilter -- NO --> discardRule[Discard Weak Rule]
    outputStrong --> checkMore{More rules to evaluate?}
    discardRule --> checkMore
    checkMore -- YES --> ruleGen
    checkMore -- NO --> stopB([STOP - Algorithm Complete])
```

### 4.2 Frequent Itemset Mining — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawDB[(Raw Transaction Database)] --> preprocess[Data Preprocessing Module]
    preprocess --> encodedDB[(Encoded Boolean Matrix)]
    encodedDB --> candGen[Candidate Generator Ck]
    candGen --> supportCounter[Support Counter via DB Scan]
    supportCounter --> freqFilter{Frequency Threshold min_sup}
    freqFilter -- Pass --> freqSet[(Frequent Itemsets Lk)]
    freqFilter -- Fail --> pruneOut[Pruned Candidate]
    freqSet --> ruleEngine[Association Rule Engine]
    ruleEngine --> confCheck{Confidence greater than or equal min_conf}
    confCheck -- Pass --> strongRule[(Strong Association Rules)]
    confCheck -- Fail --> weakRule[Discarded Weak Rules]
    strongRule --> analyst[Business Analyst / Recommendation System]
```

### 4.3 Lattice of Subsets for 3-Itemset Universe

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    level0["Empty Set: support equals D"]
    level1A["Item A: sup 4"] & level1B["Item B: sup 4"] & level1C["Item C: sup 4"]
    level2A["A B: sup 3"] & level2B["A C: sup 3"] & level2C["B C: sup 3"]
    level3["A B C: sup 2 BELOW THRESHOLD"]
    level0 --> level1A & level1B & level1C
    level1A & level1B --> level2A
    level1A & level1C --> level2B
    level1B & level1C --> level2C
    level2A & level2B & level2C --> level3
```

> [!NOTE]
> The lattice visually demonstrates the **Apriori Pruning Principle**: because node `A B C` has support 2 (below min_sup = 3), the algorithm halts growth at this branch — saving exponential computation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define the Apriori Property. How does it contribute to the efficiency of the Apriori algorithm?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Apriori Property** (also called the *anti-monotone* or *downward closure* property) states that *if an itemset is frequent, then all of its non-empty subsets must also be frequent*. Equivalently, if any subset of an itemset is infrequent, then the itemset itself is infrequent.

**Contribution to Efficiency:**
1. **Pruning** — eliminates candidate itemsets without database scans, since a candidate containing an infrequent subset can be discarded outright.
2. **Search-space reduction** — avoids the exponential blow-up of generating all $2^m - 1$ possible itemsets from an $m$-item universe.
3. **Early termination** — algorithm halts when no new frequent itemset can be formed at a given level $k$.

This drastically reduces I/O cost since fewer full database passes are required.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Differentiate between Support and Confidence with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Support | Confidence |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Frequency of an itemset in the database | Strength of implication $X \Rightarrow Y$ |
| Formula | $\text{sup}(X) = \sigma(X) / \vert D \vert$ | $\text{conf}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \text{sup}(X \cup Y) / \text{sup}(X)$ |
| Range | $[0, 1]$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| Role | Filters frequent itemsets | Filters strong rules |

**Example:** If 100 transactions contain {Tea}, and 60 of those also contain {Sugar}, then $\text{sup}(\{\text{Tea, Sugar}\}) = 60\%$, and $\text{conf}(\text{Tea} \Rightarrow \text{Sugar}) = 60/100 = 60\%$.

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]*

**Q. (a)** Given the following transactional database with $min\_sup = 50\%$ and $min\_conf = 70\%$, apply the **Apriori algorithm** step-by-step to find all frequent itemsets and generate strong association rules. **[7 Marks]**

| TID | Items |
|---|---|
| T1 | I1, I2, I5 |
| T2 | I2, I4 |
| T3 | I2, I3 |
| T4 | I1, I2, I4 |
| T5 | I1, I3 |
| T6 | I2, I3 |
| T7 | I1, I3 |
| T8 | I1, I2, I3, I5 |
| T9 | I1, I2, I3 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — $C_1$ Generation:** Count support of each item.

| Itemset | Count | Support | Frequent? |
|---|---|---|---|
| {I1} | 6 | 6/9 = 66.7% | ✅ |
| {I2} | 7 | 7/9 = 77.8% | ✅ |
| {I3} | 6 | 6/9 = 66.7% | ✅ |
| {I4} | 2 | 2/9 = 22.2% | ❌ |
| {I5} | 2 | 2/9 = 22.2% | ❌ |

$$
L_1 = \{\{I1\}, \{I2\}, \{I3\}\}
$$

**Step 2 — Generate $C_2$ via self-join of $L_1$:**

$$
C_2 = \{\{I1,I2\},\ \{I1,I3\},\ \{I2,I3\}\}
$$

| Itemset | Count | Support | Frequent? |
|---|---|---|---|
| {I1, I2} | 4 | 44.4% | ❌ |
| {I1, I3} | 4 | 44.4% | ❌ |
| {I2, I3} | 4 | 44.4% | ❌ |

$$
L_2 = \emptyset
$$

**Conclusion:** No frequent 2-itemsets exist. **Algorithm terminates.** Final frequent itemsets = $L_1 = \{\{I1\}, \{I2\}, \{I3\}\}$.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the **limitations of the Apriori algorithm** and briefly describe how the **FP-Growth algorithm** overcomes them. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Limitations of Apriori:**
1. **Candidate Generation Bottleneck** — Generates an exponential number of candidates, especially at low support thresholds (e.g., $10^{6}$ candidates for $m=30$).
2. **Multiple Database Scans** — Requires one full scan per level $k$, leading to high I/O cost on large datasets.
3. **Memory Inefficiency** — Storing $C_k$ for large $k$ consumes significant RAM.
4. **Poor Scalability** — Performance degrades sharply with increasing number of unique items and decreasing minimum support.

**FP-Growth Algorithm (Han et al., 2000):**

FP-Growth **eliminates candidate generation entirely** through a two-phase approach:

1. **FP-Tree Construction (Two database scans):**
   - First scan: identify frequent 1-itemsets and sort by descending support.
   - Second scan: construct a compact **prefix-tree (FP-Tree)** where each path represents a transaction's frequent items in descending order.

2. **Recursive Pattern Fragment Growth:**
   - For each frequent item, construct its **conditional pattern base** (set of prefix paths leading to it).
   - Build a **conditional FP-Tree** from this base.
   - Recursively mine the conditional tree to extract frequent patterns.

**Key Advantages over Apriori:**
- **No candidate generation** — only frequent patterns are extracted.
- **Compact representation** — FP-Tree is typically much smaller than the original database.
- **At most 2 database scans** — significantly reduces I/O.
- **Faster execution** — empirically 1–2 orders of magnitude faster than Apriori on dense datasets.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Alternative Choice]*

**Q. (a)** Consider a database $D$ with 10 transactions. Using $min\_sup = 30\%$ and $min\_conf = 60\%$, perform **association rule mining**. Show all $C_k$ and $L_k$ levels. **[7 Marks]**

| TID | Items |
|---|---|
| 1 | A, B, C |
| 2 | A, C |
| 3 | A, D |
| 4 | B, C, D |
| 5 | A, B, C |
| 6 | B, C |
| 7 | A, B |
| 8 | A, B, C |
| 9 | B, C |
| 10 | A, B, C |

**Model Solution:**

**$C_1$ and $L_1$:**

| Itemset | Count | Support | Frequent? |
|---|---|---|---|
| {A} | 7 | 70% | ✅ |
| {B} | 8 | 80% | ✅ |
| {C} | 8 | 80% | ✅ |
| {D} | 2 | 20% | ❌ |

$$
L_1 = \{\{A\}, \{B\}, \{C\}\}
$$

**$C_2$ and $L_2$:**

| Itemset | Count | Support | Frequent? |
|---|---|---|---|
| {A, B} | 5 | 50% | ✅ |
| {A, C} | 5 | 50% | ✅ |
| {B, C} | 7 | 70% | ✅ |

$$
L_2 = \{\{A, B\},\ \{A, C\},\ \{B, C\}\}
$$

**$C_3$ and $L_3$:**

Join $L_2$ on first item:

$$
C_3 = \{\{A, B, C\}\}
$$

Check subset pruning: {A,B}, {A,C}, {B,C} all in $L_2$ → candidate survives.

| Itemset | Count | Support | Frequent? |
|---|---|---|---|
| {A, B, C} | 3 | 30% | ✅ |

$$
L_3 = \{\{A, B, C\}\}
$$

**$C_4 = \emptyset$ → Algorithm terminates.**

**Strong Association Rules from {A, B, C}** (using $\text{conf} \geq 60\%$):

| Rule | Computation | Confidence | Strong? |
|---|---|---|---|
| A,B $\Rightarrow$ C | 3/5 | 60% | ✅ |
| A,C $\Rightarrow$ B | 3/5 | 60% | ✅ |
| B,C $\Rightarrow$ A | 3/8 | 37.5% | ❌ |
| A $\Rightarrow$ B,C | 3/7 | 42.9% | ❌ |
| B $\Rightarrow$ A,C | 3/8 | 37.5% | ❌ |
| C $\Rightarrow$ A,B | 3/8 | 37.5% | ❌ |

**Strong rules:** $A,B \Rightarrow C$ and $A,C \Rightarrow B$.

---

**Q. (b)** What is **Lift** in association rule mining? Why is it considered a better measure than confidence? Compute the lift of rule $B \Rightarrow C$ from the database above. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of Lift:**
Lift measures the **statistical dependence** between the antecedent and consequent of a rule, correcting for the baseline frequency of the consequent.

$$
\text{lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{\text{sup}(X \cup Y)}{\text{sup}(X) \cdot \text{sup}(Y)} = \frac{\text{conf}(X \Rightarrow Y)}{\text{sup}(Y)}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- $\text{lift} > 1$: $X$ and $Y$ are **positively correlated** (occurrence of $X$ promotes $Y$).
- $\text{lift} = 1$: $X$ and $Y$ are **independent**.
- $\text{lift} < 1$: $X$ and $Y$ are **negatively correlated**.

**Why Lift is Better than Confidence:**
Confidence does not account for the **base popularity** of the consequent. A rule like "People who buy bread $\Rightarrow$ buy milk" may have high confidence simply because milk is popular — not because bread causes milk purchases. Lift normalizes against this baseline, revealing genuine correlation.

**Computation for $B \Rightarrow C$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{sup}(B \cup C) &= 7/10 = 0.70 \\
\text{sup}(B) &= 8/10 = 0.80 \\
\text{sup}(C) &= 8/10 = 0.80 \\
\text{lift}(B \Rightarrow C) &= \frac{0.70}{0.80 \times 0.80} = \frac{0.70}{0.64} = 1.09375
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Since $\text{lift} > 1$, items $B$ and $C$ are **slightly positively correlated** — they co-occur more often than by chance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert counts to fractions** — Students often write support as a raw count instead of a ratio. Always express as $\sigma / \vert D \vert$.
> 2. **Skipping the pruning step** — Examiners deduct 1–2 marks when $C_3$ candidates are listed without verifying the Apriori Property.
> 3. **Confusing support count with support** — $\sigma(X)$ is a count; $\text{sup}(X)$ is a fraction. Do not interchange.
> 4. **Ignoring Lift** — Many students stop after generating strong rules. KTU 2024 Scheme expects the interpretation of lift in at least one sub-part.
> 5. **Missing the symmetry pitfall** — A strong rule $X \Rightarrow Y$ does *not* imply $Y \Rightarrow X$ is also strong. Always compute confidence independently for both directions.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Frequent Itemset Mining** finds itemsets whose support $\geq min\_sup$.
- ✅ **Apriori Property (Downward Closure):** All subsets of a frequent itemset must also be frequent.
- ✅ **Apriori Algorithm** alternates between **candidate generation** ($C_k$) and **frequent set identification** ($L_k$).
- ✅ **Support** = $\sigma(X) / \vert D \vert$; **Confidence** = $\text{sup}(X \cup Y) / \text{sup}(X)$.
- ✅ **Lift** $> 1$ implies positive correlation; $= 1$ independence; $< 1$ negative correlation.
- ✅ **Strong rule** = frequent itemset + confidence $\geq min\_conf$.
- ✅ **Rule generation** considers all non-empty proper subsets of each frequent itemset.
- ✅ **FP-Growth** overcomes Apriori's bottleneck by avoiding candidate generation via FP-Tree construction (max 2 DB scans).
- ✅ **Anti-monotonicity** is the theoretical heart of efficient pruning.
- ✅ Always show **$C_k$, $L_k$, and rule derivation** in exam answers — partial solutions lose marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Market Based Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT751 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Market Based Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Market Based Model (MBM)** in Data Analytics is a strategic analytics framework where the design, execution, and evaluation of analytical models are driven primarily by **external market signals** rather than purely internal organizational needs. It is one of the three recognized analytics maturity archetypes in the KTU PEMUT751 syllabus, sitting alongside the *Need Based Model* and the *Hybrid (Need-Market) Model*.

In a Market Based Model, the analytics pipeline sources its data from:
- **External market data** (industry trends, sector benchmarks, macroeconomic indicators).
- **Competitive intelligence** (rival product performance, pricing strategies, market share shifts).
- **Customer/transaction ecosystems** (third-party datasets, social media sentiment, market basket co-occurrences).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"A Market Based Model is an analytics approach in which the model parameters, features, and success metrics are determined by prevailing market conditions, competitive benchmarks, and external data sources, rather than being dictated solely by internal operational requirements."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Weather Forecaster vs. The Home Gardener"

Imagine two farmers deciding what to plant:

| Farmer Type | Decision Basis | Analogy to Analytics |
|-------------|---------------|----------------------|
| **Home Gardener** (Need-Based) | Decides based on family food preferences | Analytics driven by internal KPIs |
| **Weather Forecaster Farmer** (Market-Based) | Studies market prices, weather data, competitor crop choices before deciding | Analytics driven by external market data |
| **Smart Hybrid Farmer** | Balances both family needs and market signals | Hybrid Need-Market Model |

> [!NOTE]
> The Market Based Farmer does **not** ignore personal needs entirely, but the **dominant driver** of every decision (what to plant, when, how much, and to whom to sell) is the **external market reality**.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics Used in a Market Based Model

- **Support** — frequency of an itemset in the market transactions.
- **Confidence** — conditional probability of purchase.
- **Lift** — strength of association over random chance.
- **Market Beta (β)** — sensitivity of an asset's return to market returns.
- **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** — projected revenue from a customer segment.

All metrics are computed on **aggregated external transaction databases** rather than internal silo data.

## 1.4 Characteristics of a Market Based Model

- **Externally Oriented** — features derived from competitor, industry, and macroeconomic data.
- **Demand-Sensitive** — model outputs react to shifts in market demand rather than internal supply constraints.
- **Benchmark-Driven** — model accuracy is measured against industry standards and rival performance.
- **Customer-Centric** — heavy reliance on customer behavior patterns observed in the broader market.
- **Dynamic Recalibration** — parameters updated frequently as market conditions evolve.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Logic of a Market Based Model

The operational flow of a Market Based Model follows these structured steps:

1. **External Data Ingestion** — collect market data feeds (industry reports, competitor pricing APIs, social signals).
2. **Market Feature Engineering** — derive market-sensitive variables (competitor index, demand elasticity, sector volatility).
3. **Model Parameterization** — set model coefficients using market-driven priors or benchmarks.
4. **Prediction/Association Engine** — run algorithms such as Market Basket Analysis, CAPM regression, or competitive clustering.
5. **Market-Aligned Validation** — evaluate model using market-grounded KPIs (e.g., Market Lift, Share-of-Voice parity).
6. **Strategic Output** — recommendations for pricing, positioning, and product placement aligned to the prevailing market.

> [!NOTE]
> The critical philosophical shift in MBM is in **Step 2** — features are *engineered from the outside world*, not from internal operational logs.

## 2.2 Core Components of the Market Based Model

### A. Market Basket Analysis (MBA) — The Workhorse
Market Basket Analysis is the most frequently tested MBM technique in KTU examinations. It identifies associations between items purchased together.

### B. Market Mix Modelling (MMM)
A regression-based approach that quantifies the impact of marketing inputs (TV, digital, print) on sales, using market-level aggregated data.

### C. Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) — Financial MBM
A foundational financial analytics model that prices an asset based on its relationship to the overall market.

### D. Competitive Positioning Analytics
Uses external benchmarks to position an organization within the market landscape.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Typical Use |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | $Support(A) = \dfrac{\text{Transactions containing } A}{\text{Total Transactions}}$ | $A$ = itemset | MBA — frequency of itemset |
| 2 | $Support(A \Rightarrow B) = \dfrac{\text{Txn containing } A \text{ and } B}{\text{Total Txn}}$ | $A, B$ = itemsets | Joint occurrence |
| 3 | $Confidence(A \Rightarrow B) = \dfrac{Support(A \Rightarrow B)}{Support(A)}$ | Conditional probability | Rule reliability |
| 4 | $Lift(A \Rightarrow B) = \dfrac{Support(A \Rightarrow B)}{Support(A) \times Support(B)}$ | Correlation indicator | Strength of rule |
| 5 | $E[R_i] = R_f + \beta_i (E[R_m] - R_f)$ | $R_f$ = risk-free rate, $R_m$ = market return, $\beta_i$ = beta | CAPM expected return |
| 6 | $CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{(Revenue_t - Cost_t) \times r_t}{(1 + d)^t}$ | $r_t$ = retention, $d$ = discount | Customer lifetime value |
| 7 | $\text{Market Share} = \dfrac{\text{Company Sales}}{\text{Total Market Sales}} \times 100$ | Percentage | Competitive KPI |
| 8 | $\text{Price Elasticity} = \dfrac{\%\Delta Q_d}{\%\Delta P}$ | $Q_d$ = quantity demanded, $P$ = price | Demand sensitivity |
| 9 | $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Standard score | Market normalization |
| 10 | $\text{NPS} = \% \text{Promoters} - \% \text{Detractors}$ | Net Promoter Score | Market sentiment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lift interpretation (Board favourite):**
> - $Lift > 1$ → Positive association (items bought together more than random).
> - $Lift = 1$ → Independent items.
> - $Lift < 1$ → Negative association (substitutes).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Retail & E-Commerce** — Amazon, Flipkart, BigBasket deploy MBA for "Frequently Bought Together" recommendations.
- **Banking & Finance** — CAPM is used in portfolio analytics to price equities.
- **Telecommunications** — Customer churn and CLV models are calibrated using competitor market data.
- **Manufacturing** — Market Mix Modelling allocates multi-million rupee advertising budgets.
- **Healthcare Analytics** — Market-level epidemiological data drives drug demand forecasting.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Market Basket Analysis: Support, Confidence, Lift

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup
A supermarket database has **5000 transactions** in a given week. From these transactions:
- **Bread** appears in **1500** transactions.
- **Butter** appears in **1000** transactions.
- **Bread AND Butter** appear together in **750** transactions.

Compute **Support, Confidence, and Lift** for the rule: *If a customer buys Bread, they will also buy Butter.*

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Support of Bread**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Support(\text{Bread}) &= \dfrac{\text{Transactions containing Bread}}{\text{Total Transactions}} \\
&= \dfrac{1500}{5000} \\
&= 0.30 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 30\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Support of Butter**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Support(\text{Butter}) &= \dfrac{\text{Transactions containing Butter}}{\text{Total Transactions}} \\
&= \dfrac{1000}{5000} \\
&= 0.20 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 20\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Joint Support (Bread and Butter)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Support(\text{Bread} \Rightarrow \text{Butter}) &= \dfrac{\text{Txn containing Bread AND Butter}}{\text{Total Txn}} \\
&= \dfrac{750}{5000} \\
&= 0.15 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 15\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Confidence of the Rule**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Confidence(\text{Bread} \Rightarrow \text{Butter}) &= \dfrac{Support(\text{Bread} \Rightarrow \text{Butter})}{Support(\text{Bread})} \\
&= \dfrac{0.15}{0.30} \\
&= 0.50 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Out of all customers who bought Bread, **50%** also bought Butter. This is the conditional probability $P(\text{Butter} \mid \text{Bread})$.

**Step 5 — Compute Lift of the Rule**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Lift(\text{Bread} \Rightarrow \text{Butter}) &= \dfrac{Support(\text{Bread} \Rightarrow \text{Butter})}{Support(\text{Bread}) \times Support(\text{Butter})} \\
&= \dfrac{0.15}{0.30 \times 0.20} \\
&= \dfrac{0.15}{0.06} \\
&= 2.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Since $Lift = 2.5 > 1$, customers who buy Bread are **2.5 times more likely** to buy Butter compared to a random customer. This is a *strong positive association*, validating the Market Based Model recommendation to place Bread and Butter near each other or bundle them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always state the Lift interpretation explicitly. Examiners award a full mark for the concluding sentence relating Lift to a business decision.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — CAPM Expected Return

### 3.2.1 Given Data
- Risk-free rate $R_f = 6\%$
- Expected market return $E[R_m] = 14\%$
- Stock's market beta $\beta_i = 1.4$

Find the expected return on the stock using the Capital Asset Pricing Model.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the CAPM equation**

$$
E[R_i] = R_f + \beta_i \big( E[R_m] - R_f \big)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Market Risk Premium**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[R_m] - R_f &= 14\% - 6\% \\
&= 8\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply the Market Risk Premium by Beta**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta_i \times (E[R_m] - R_f) &= 1.4 \times 8\% \\
&= 11.2\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Add the Risk-Free Rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[R_i] &= 6\% + 11.2\% \\
&= 17.2\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The market-based model expects this stock to deliver **17.2%** return. The high beta (1.4 > 1) means the stock is **more volatile** than the market, amplifying both gains and losses.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)

### 3.3.1 Given Data
- Annual revenue per customer = ₹5000
- Annual servicing cost = ₹1500
- Retention rate = 80% (decay factor)
- Discount rate = 10%
- Customer horizon = 3 years

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Annual Profit per Customer**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Profit} &= \text{Revenue} - \text{Cost} \\
&= 5000 - 1500 \\
&= 3500 \;\; \text{INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Retention Decay and Discounting**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CLV &= \sum_{t=1}^{3} \dfrac{3500 \times (0.8)^t}{(1 + 0.10)^t} \\
&= \dfrac{3500 \times 0.8}{1.1} + \dfrac{3500 \times 0.64}{1.21} + \dfrac{3500 \times 0.512}{1.331}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate Each Year**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Year 1} &= \dfrac{2800}{1.1} = 2545.45 \\
\text{Year 2} &= \dfrac{2240}{1.21} = 1851.24 \\
\text{Year 3} &= \dfrac{1792}{1.331} = 1346.36
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the Discounted Profits**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CLV &= 2545.45 + 1851.24 + 1346.36 \\
&= 5743.05 \;\; \text{INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Each customer in this market segment is worth approximately **₹5,743** over 3 years. The market-based model would recommend acquiring similar customers as long as Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) is below this CLV.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Dimension | Need Based Model | Market Based Model | Hybrid Model |
|-----------|------------------|--------------------|--------------|
| Primary Data Source | Internal operational data | External market & competitor data | Both internal & external |
| Decision Driver | Organizational requirements | Market conditions | Balanced |
| Responsiveness | Slow to market shifts | Highly responsive | Adaptive |
| Computational Cost | Low | Moderate–High | High |
| Typical Techniques | Internal KPI dashboards, OLAP | MBA, CAPM, MMM, CLV | Combined pipelines |
| KTU Board Reference | Module 2 / Module 3 | Module 3 (Current Topic) | Module 3 |
| Risk | Misses market opportunities | Ignores internal constraints | Complexity overhead |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Market Based Model Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Market Data Sources] --> B[Data Ingestion Layer]
    B --> C[Market Feature Engineering]
    C --> D[Model Parameterization with Market Priors]
    D --> E[Analytical Engine]
    E --> F[Market Basket Analysis Module]
    E --> G[CAPM Regression Module]
    E --> H[Customer Lifetime Value Module]
    E --> I[Market Mix Model Module]
    F --> J[Strategic Output Layer]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Pricing Decisions]
    J --> L[Product Positioning]
    J --> M[Investment Allocation]
    J --> N[Customer Targeting]
```

## 4.2 Market Basket Analysis — Detailed Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Txn1[Raw Transaction Log] --> Clean[Data Cleaning]
    Clean --> Items[Itemset Generation]
    Items --> Freq[Frequent Itemset Mining Apriori FP Growth]
    Freq --> Rules[Association Rule Generation]
    Rules --> Eval[Support Confidence Lift Evaluation]
    Eval --> Valid{Lift greater than 1?}
    Valid -- Yes --> Strong[Strong Rule]
    Valid -- No --> Weak[Weak Rule]
    Strong --> Output[Recommendation Engine]
    Weak --> Discard[Discarded]
```

## 4.3 CAPM Decision Flow in Market Based Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Identify Asset] --> RfGather[Collect Risk Free Rate]
    RfGather --> RmGather[Collect Market Return]
    RmGather --> BetaGather[Compute Asset Beta]
    BetaGather --> Formula[Apply CAPM Equation]
    Formula --> Compute[Compute Expected Return]
    Compute --> Compare{Return exceeds Hurdle Rate?}
    Compare -- Yes --> Invest[Recommend Investment]
    Compare -- No --> Reject[Reject Asset]
```

## 4.4 Positioning of Market Based Model in Analytics Maturity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Level 1 Need Based Internal KPIs] --> L2[Level 2 Market Based External Signals]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 Hybrid Need Market Balanced]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4 Predictive Prescriptive Analytics]
```

## 4.5 Data Flow Architecture for Market Based Analytics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ExternalSources [External Data Universe]
        S1[Competitor Pricing APIs]
        S2[Industry Reports]
        S3[Social Media Sentiment]
        S4[Macroeconomic Indicators]
    end

    subgraph ProcessingLayer [Processing Pipeline]
        P1[Data Lake Storage]
        P2[ETL and Transformation]
        P3[Feature Engineering Module]
    end

    subgraph ModelLayer [Model Layer]
        M1[Association Mining]
        M2[Regression Models]
        M3[Clustering and Segmentation]
        M4[Time Series Forecasting]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer [Strategic Outputs]
        O1[Market Aligned Recommendations]
        O2[Benchmark Reports]
        O3[Customer Strategy Adjustments]
    end

    S1 --> P1
    S2 --> P1
    S3 --> P1
    S4 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> M1
    P3 --> M2
    P3 --> M3
    P3 --> M4
    M1 --> O1
    M2 --> O1
    M3 --> O2
    M4 --> O3
```

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid diagrams above use **alphanumeric node IDs** only. No reserved keywords are used as node names. No markdown formatting is embedded inside node labels.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the Market Based Model in the context of Data Analytics. List any two characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**
The Market Based Model is an analytics approach in which the model's features, parameters, and success criteria are derived primarily from **external market data**, competitive benchmarks, and industry-wide signals, rather than being driven solely by internal organizational needs.

**Two Characteristics:**
1. **Externally Oriented** — the model depends on data sourced from outside the organization (competitor pricing, market share, macroeconomic data).
2. **Benchmark-Driven Validation** — model performance is measured against industry standards and rival performance rather than internal KPIs only.

*(1 mark for the definition, 1 mark per characteristic — total 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between Support, Confidence, and Lift in Market Basket Analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

| Metric | Definition | Formula | Purpose |
|--------|-----------|---------|---------|
| **Support** | Fraction of transactions containing the itemset | $\dfrac{\text{Txn with itemset}}{\text{Total Txn}}$ | Filters frequent itemsets |
| **Confidence** | Conditional probability of consequent given antecedent | $\dfrac{Support(A \Rightarrow B)}{Support(A)}$ | Measures rule reliability |
| **Lift** | Ratio of observed co-occurrence to expected co-occurrence | $\dfrac{Support(A \Rightarrow B)}{Support(A) \times Support(B)}$ | Measures strength of association |

*(1 mark per row — total 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**A supermarket chain maintains a transaction database of 8000 weekly entries. The following counts are observed:**
- Customers buying **Cereal** = 2400
- Customers buying **Milk** = 3200
- Customers buying **both Cereal and Milk** = 1200

**(a)** Compute the Support, Confidence, and Lift for the rule *Cereal → Milk*. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Interpret the Lift value obtained and explain its business significance in the context of a Market Based Model. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**Given Data:**
- Total transactions $N = 8000$
- $Support(\text{Cereal}) = \dfrac{2400}{8000}$
- $Support(\text{Milk}) = \dfrac{3200}{8000}$
- $Support(\text{Cereal} \cap \text{Milk}) = \dfrac{1200}{8000}$

**Step 1 — Compute Support of Cereal**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Support(\text{Cereal}) &= \dfrac{2400}{8000} \\
&= 0.30 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 30\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Support of Milk**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Support(\text{Milk}) &= \dfrac{3200}{8000} \\
&= 0.40 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 40\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Joint Support**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Support(\text{Cereal} \Rightarrow \text{Milk}) &= \dfrac{1200}{8000} \\
&= 0.15 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 15\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Confidence**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Confidence(\text{Cereal} \Rightarrow \text{Milk}) &= \dfrac{0.15}{0.30} \\
&= 0.50 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Lift**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Lift(\text{Cereal} \Rightarrow \text{Milk}) &= \dfrac{0.15}{0.30 \times 0.40} \\
&= \dfrac{0.15}{0.12} \\
&= 1.25
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Marks: 1.5 for each metric + 1 for correct formulas = 7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**Interpretation of Lift = 1.25:**

Since $Lift = 1.25 > 1$, there exists a **positive association** between Cereal and Milk purchases. A customer who buys Cereal is **1.25 times more likely** to also buy Milk than a randomly selected customer.

**Business Significance in a Market Based Model:**

1. **Cross-Selling Strategy** — The supermarket can place Cereal and Milk in adjacent aisles or design promotional bundles to leverage this association.
2. **Inventory Optimization** — Stocking levels of Cereal and Milk can be coordinated since their demand is correlated.
3. **Recommendation Engine** — Online grocery platforms can recommend Milk to customers who add Cereal to their cart, increasing basket size.
4. **Market-Aligned Pricing** — Dynamic discount on one item can boost sales of the associated item, aligning with market basket behaviour.
5. **Strategic Decisioning** — Since the model is **market-based** (built on actual transaction data of the wider market), the recommendations are externally validated and reflect genuine customer purchasing patterns.

*(7 marks: 2 for lift interpretation, 5 for business significance with at least 3 points)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) as a Market Based Model. Derive the expected return for a stock given $R_f = 7\%$, $E[R_m] = 15\%$, and $\beta = 1.2$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Market Based Analytics Model. List four limitations of relying solely on a Market Based Model. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**Explanation of CAPM as a Market Based Model:**
The Capital Asset Pricing Model is a foundational **financial analytics** model that prices an asset based on its sensitivity (beta) to overall **market movements**. It is a quintessential Market Based Model because:
- The expected return is anchored to the **external market return** $E[R_m]$.
- The risk-free rate $R_f$ is an **external macroeconomic input**.
- Beta $\beta$ is computed by regressing the asset's historical returns against the market index — purely **market-driven data**.

**Derivation of Expected Return:**

**Step 1 — Identify the CAPM Formula**

$$
E[R_i] = R_f + \beta_i \big( E[R_m] - R_f \big)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Market Risk Premium**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[R_m] - R_f &= 15\% - 7\% \\
&= 8\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by Beta**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta \times (E[R_m] - R_f) &= 1.2 \times 8\% \\
&= 9.6\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Add the Risk-Free Rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[R_i] &= 7\% + 9.6\% \\
&= 16.6\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The expected return on the stock is **16.6%**, which is higher than the market return of 15% because its beta (1.2) indicates it is **more volatile than the market**.

*(7 marks: 3 for CAPM explanation, 4 for the derivation)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**Architecture Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Market Data] --> B[Data Acquisition]
    B --> C[Preprocessing and Cleansing]
    C --> D[Feature Engineering]
    D --> E[Model Selection and Training]
    E --> F[Market Based Models MBA CAPM MMM CLV]
    F --> G[Validation Against Market Benchmarks]
    G --> H[Deployment]
    H --> I[Strategic Market Decisions]
```

**Four Limitations of a Pure Market Based Model:**

1. **Ignores Internal Constraints** — A pure MBM may recommend strategies that the organization cannot operationally execute (e.g., insufficient production capacity).
2. **Data Reliability Risk** — External market data may be noisy, outdated, or manipulated, leading to flawed analytics.
3. **Over-Sensitivity to Market Volatility** — Models like CAPM produce fluctuating outputs during market turbulence, making planning difficult.
4. **Lack of Internal Context** — Employee skill levels, organizational culture, and proprietary processes are ignored, reducing implementation feasibility.

*(7 marks: 3 for diagram, 4 for limitations — 1 mark each)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not** confuse *Market Basket Analysis* (a market-based model technique) with *Market Based Model* (the strategic framework). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.
> - In Lift computations, **always** state the business interpretation explicitly. A numeric answer without interpretation fetches only 60% of the marks.
> - For CAPM questions, **show the Market Risk Premium step separately**. Many students skip this and lose 1 mark.
> - **Do not** write formulas with vertical bars `|` inside the table — always use `\` (LaTeX) formatting in numerical sections to avoid markdown table breakage.
> - In architecture diagrams, use **alphanumeric node IDs** only (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`). Using `end` as a node name causes Mermaid compilation failure and 0 marks for the diagram.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Market Based Model (MBM)** is a strategic analytics framework driven by **external market data** and competitive intelligence.
- It is one of three maturity archetypes: *Need Based*, *Market Based*, and *Hybrid*.
- The **most tested technique** in MBM is **Market Basket Analysis** — memorize Support, Confidence, and Lift.
- **Lift > 1** indicates a *positive association*; **Lift = 1** indicates *independence*; **Lift < 1** indicates a *negative/substitute relationship*.
- **CAPM** is the financial-analytics face of MBM: $E[R_i] = R_f + \beta (E[R_m] - R_f)$.
- **CLV** uses market-observed retention and discount rates to value a customer.
- **Market Mix Models (MMM)** allocate marketing budgets based on market-level aggregated data.
- MBM is **externally oriented**, **benchmark-driven**, **demand-sensitive**, and **dynamically recalibrated**.
- **Limitations** include ignoring internal constraints, data quality issues, market volatility sensitivity, and lack of organizational context.
- **KTU High-Yield Keywords:** *External data, Benchmark, Association rule, Market Premium, Beta, Lift, Cross-selling, Competitive intelligence.*
- Always **end** an MBM solution with a **business interpretation** — this is where the extra 1–2 marks are awarded.
- **Hybrid Model** (Need + Market) is the recommended end-state in analytics maturity, balancing internal capability with external opportunity.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Association and Sequential Rule Mining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT751 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Association and Sequential Rule Mining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Association Rule Mining (ARM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> *Association Rule Mining* is an unsupervised data mining technique that discovers interesting, hidden correlations, frequent patterns, associations, or causal structures among sets of items (or variables) in large transactional databases. Formally, given a set of transactions $T = \{t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_N\}$ where each transaction $t_i \subseteq I$ (the universe of items), an association rule is an implication of the form $X \Rightarrow Y$, where $X, Y \subseteq I$, $X \cap Y = \emptyset$, and $X, Y \neq \emptyset$.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Supermarket Basket 🛒

Imagine you walk into a **Reliance Smart** store in Kerala with a basket. You pick up **Bread, Butter, and Milk** together. The store manager watches millions of such baskets via POS data and notices a striking pattern: *75% of customers who buy Bread also buy Butter*. Suddenly, the manager gains strategic insight — he can:

1. **Place Bread and Butter on adjacent shelves** to boost cross-selling.
2. **Discount Butter** to lure Bread buyers into buying more profitable items.
3. **Bundle them as a combo offer** during Onam festivals.

This is precisely *Association Rule Mining* — it answers the question: *"If a customer buys item-set X, what else are they likely to buy?"*

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context**: The problem was formally introduced by **Rakesh Agrawal, Tomasz Imieliński, and Arun Swami** in their landmark 1993 paper *"Mining Association Rules between Sets of Items in Large Databases"* at SIGMOD. The Apriori algorithm (Agrawal & Srikant, 1994) remains the foundational baseline taught in KTU curricula.

## 1.2 Sequential Rule Mining (SRM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition**
> *Sequential Pattern Mining* is the discovery of statistically relevant subsequences (ordered sequences) in a sequence database where temporal, positional, or logical ordering matters. Unlike ARM, which treats each transaction as a *set* (order within a basket irrelevant), SRM treats each customer's transactions as an *ordered list* (sequence) of itemsets, capturing the **order in which items are purchased**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Netflix Binge-Watch Pattern 📺

Think about your own Netflix behaviour:
1. Monday: You watch **"Stranger Things S1"**
2. Tuesday: You watch **"Stranger Things S2"**
3. Wednesday: You watch **"Dark"**

Netflix's recommender detects that users who watch *Stranger Things S1* frequently follow it up with *S2* (within 7 days), then transition to *Dark*. This is a **sequential pattern**: $\langle(\text{StrangerThings S1})(\text{StrangerThings S2})(\text{Dark})\rangle$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from ARM**
> In ARM: $\{Bread, Butter\}$ and $\{Butter, Bread\}$ are the *same* basket.
> In SRM: $\langle Bread, Butter \rangle$ (Bread first, Butter later) and $\langle Butter, Bread \rangle$ (Butter first, Bread later) are *different* sequential patterns.

## 1.3 The Universal Notation of Transactions

$$T = \{t_1, t_2, t_3, \ldots, t_N\}$$

where each transaction $t_i$ is a subset of items drawn from the universe $I = \{i_1, i_2, \ldots, i_M\}$. The total number of transactions is the cardinality **N** (often denoted $|D|$ or $|T|$).

## 1.4 Why Does This Topic Matter to KTU 2024 Engineers?

| Industry Domain | Real-World ARM/SRM Application |
|-----------------|-------------------------------|
| **Retail & E-Commerce** (Flipkart, Amazon) | Cross-selling, recommendation engines |
| **Banking & Finance** | Fraud pattern detection, credit card usage sequences |
| **Bioinformatics** | DNA sequence motif discovery |
| **Telecom (Jio, Airtel)** | Customer churn prediction via usage sequences |
| **Web Usage Mining** | Clickstream analysis, page navigation patterns |
| **Healthcare** | Disease progression sequences (symptom → diagnosis → treatment) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Quality Metrics — The "Holy Trinity"

For any association rule $X \Rightarrow Y$, three metrics quantify its *interestingness*:

### 2.1.1 Support (Frequency)

$$s(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{\sigma(X \cup Y)}{N}$$

**Why it matters:** Measures how *popular* the rule is across the database. A rule occurring only twice in a million transactions is statistically meaningless noise.

**Engineering Intuition:** Think of support as the *sample size* in a statistical experiment — too small, and your conclusion is unreliable.

### 2.1.2 Confidence (Strength)

$$c(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{\sigma(X \cup Y)}{\sigma(X)} = \frac{s(X \cup Y)}{s(X)}$$

**Why it matters:** Measures the *reliability* of the inference. If 90% of Bread buyers also buy Butter, the rule *Bread ⇒ Butter* has high predictive power.

**Engineering Intuition:** Confidence is essentially the **conditional probability** $P(Y \mid X)$ — a direct application of Bayes' theorem intuition.

### 2.1.3 Lift (Correlation / Interest)

$$\text{Lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{c(X \Rightarrow Y)}{s(Y)} = \frac{s(X \cup Y)}{s(X) \cdot s(Y)}$$

**Why it matters:** Confidence alone is misleading. A rule can have high confidence simply because $Y$ is universally popular. Lift corrects for this baseline by measuring how much more likely $Y$ occurs *given* $X$ versus the random baseline.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lift Interpretation Rule**
> - $\text{Lift} > 1$: Positive correlation ($X$ and $Y$ appear together *more* than chance)
> - $\text{Lift} = 1$: Independence ($X$ and $Y$ are statistically unrelated)
> - $\text{Lift} < 1$: Negative correlation ($X$ and $Y$ repel each other)

### 2.1.4 Conviction (Directional Asymmetry)

$$\text{Conv}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{1 - s(Y)}{1 - c(X \Rightarrow Y)}$$

**Why it matters:** Conviction captures the *direction* of implication. $\text{Conv}(X \Rightarrow Y) \neq \text{Conv}(Y \Rightarrow X)$ in general, unlike Lift which is symmetric. It also handles the edge case $c = 1$ gracefully (conviction becomes $\infty$).

## 2.2 The Apriori Property (Anti-Monotonicity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Apriori Property (Foundation Stone of the Algorithm)**
> *All non-empty subsets of a frequent itemset must also be frequent.* Equivalently, if an itemset $I$ has $s(I) < \text{min\_sup}$, then *any superset* $J \supseteq I$ must also have $s(J) < \text{min\_sup}$.

**Why this works mathematically:** Support is **anti-monotonic** with respect to set inclusion:

$$A \subseteq B \implies s(A) \geq s(B)$$

This single property enables a *pruning* strategy that reduces the search space from $2^M$ (exponential) to a manageable subset.

## 2.3 The Downward Closure Lemma

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Statement**: Let $\mathcal{F}$ denote the family of all frequent itemsets. Then $\mathcal{F}$ is a *downward-closed* (or *hereditary*) family: if $X \in \mathcal{F}$ and $Y \subseteq X$, then $Y \in \mathcal{F}$. This is the dual statement of the Apriori property.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Formula | Range | Interpretation |
|--------|---------|-------|----------------|
| **Support** $s$ | $\sigma(X \cup Y) / N$ | $[0, 1]$ | Fraction of transactions containing both $X$ and $Y$ |
| **Confidence** $c$ | $\sigma(X \cup Y) / \sigma(X)$ | $[0, 1]$ | Conditional probability $P(Y \mid X)$ |
| **Lift** | $s(X \cup Y) / [s(X) \cdot s(Y)]$ | $[0, \infty)$ | $\;>\!1$ positive, $=\!1$ independent, $\;<\!1$ negative |
| **Conviction** | $[1 - s(Y)] / [1 - c(X \Rightarrow Y)]$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Higher $\Rightarrow$ stronger implication |
| **Leverage** | $s(X \cup Y) - s(X) \cdot s(Y)$ | $[-1, 1]$ | Difference of observed vs. expected co-occurrence |
| **All-Confidence** | $s(X \cup Y) / \max\{\sigma(X), \sigma(Y)\}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Lower bound on interestingness (used in hyperclique patterns) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Property: Conviction Bound**
> When $c(X \Rightarrow Y) = 1$, the denominator becomes 0 and conviction $\to \infty$. This correctly signals a *perfect logical implication* — every occurrence of $X$ guarantees $Y$.

## 2.5 Closed and Maximal Frequent Itemsets

> [!NOTE]
> - **Maximal Frequent Itemset**: A frequent itemset $X$ such that no *proper* superset of $X$ is frequent. Eliminates redundant frequent sets but loses support information.
> - **Closed Frequent Itemset**: A frequent itemset $X$ such that no proper superset of $X$ has the *same support count*. Preserves support info. — *preferred in KTU exams*.

## 2.6 The FP-Growth Improvement

The **Frequent Pattern Growth (FP-Growth)** algorithm (Han, Pei, Yin, 2000) avoids the costly candidate-generation-and-test cycle of Apriori by:
1. Compressing the database into an **FP-Tree** (a prefix-tree structure).
2. Mining patterns via *conditional pattern bases* and *conditional FP-trees*.

**Key Advantage:** Only **2 database scans** versus $k$ scans in Apriori for $k$-itemsets.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Reference Transactional Database

Consider the following transactional database $D$ (the *workhorse example* used in KTU model answers):

| TID | Items Purchased |
|-----|----------------|
| T100 | {Bread, Butter, Milk, Diapers} |
| T200 | {Bread, Butter, Diapers} |
| T300 | {Butter, Milk} |
| T400 | {Bread, Milk, Diapers} |
| T500 | {Butter, Milk, Diapers} |

**Parameters (Assumed):**
- Total transactions $N = 5$
- $\text{min\_sup} = 60\% \;\; (\Rightarrow \text{min\_sup\_count} = 3)$
- $\text{min\_conf} = 70\%$

## 3.2 Detailed Computation — C₁ and L₁

### Step 1: Generate Candidate 1-Itemsets $C_1$

Each individual item is a candidate 1-itemset:

$$C_1 = \{\{Bread\}, \{Butter\}, \{Milk\}, \{Diapers\}\}$$

### Step 2: Scan Database to Compute Support Counts

Count how many transactions contain each item:

$$\sigma(\{Bread\}) = |\{T100, T200, T400\}| = 3$$

$$\sigma(\{Butter\}) = |\{T100, T200, T300, T500\}| = 4$$

$$\sigma(\{Milk\}) = |\{T100, T300, T400, T500\}| = 4$$

$$\sigma(\{Diapers\}) = |\{T100, T200, T400, T500\}| = 4$$

### Step 3: Prune Against min_sup_count = 3

| 1-Itemset | Support Count | Support % | Frequent? |
|-----------|---------------|-----------|-----------|
| {Bread} | 3 | 60% | ✓ |
| {Butter} | 4 | 80% | ✓ |
| {Milk} | 4 | 80% | ✓ |
| {Diapers} | 4 | 80% | ✓ |

$$L_1 = \{\{Bread\}, \{Butter\}, \{Milk\}, \{Diapers\}\} \;\; \text{(all pass)}$$

## 3.3 Detailed Computation — C₂ and L₂

### Step 4: Self-Join $L_1 \bowtie L_1$ to Generate $C_2$

The combinatorial count of 2-subsets from a 4-element $L_1$ is $\binom{4}{2} = 6$:

$$C_2 = \{\{B, Bu\}, \{B, M\}, \{B, D\}, \{Bu, M\}, \{Bu, D\}, \{M, D\}\}$$

*(Using abbreviations B = Bread, Bu = Butter, M = Milk, D = Diapers.)*

### Step 5: Count Support via Database Scan

| 2-Itemset | Appears in | Count | Frequent? |
|-----------|------------|-------|-----------|
| {Bread, Butter} | T100, T200 | 2 | ✗ |
| {Bread, Milk} | T100, T400 | 2 | ✗ |
| {Bread, Diapers} | T100, T200, T400 | **3** | ✓ |
| {Butter, Milk} | T100, T300, T500 | **3** | ✓ |
| {Butter, Diapers} | T100, T200, T500 | **3** | ✓ |
| {Milk, Diapers} | T100, T400, T500 | **3** | ✓ |

$$L_2 = \{\{B, D\}, \{Bu, M\}, \{Bu, D\}, \{M, D\}\}$$

## 3.4 Detailed Computation — C₃ and L₃ (Apriori Pruning in Action)

### Step 6: Self-Join $L_2 \bowtie L_2$ to Generate $C_3$

Sorting items lexicographically, we generate candidates by joining itemsets sharing a $(k-2)$-prefix:

$$C_3 = \{\{B, Bu, D\}, \{B, M, D\}, \{Bu, M, D\}\}$$

### Step 7: Apply Apriori Pruning (Subset Test)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Apriori Property Application**: A candidate $k$-itemset can only be frequent if *all* its $(k-1)$-subsets are frequent. We check every 2-subset of each candidate:

**Candidate {Bread, Butter, Diapers}:**
- 2-subset {Bread, Butter} $\notin L_2$ $\Rightarrow$ **Pruned** ✓

**Candidate {Bread, Milk, Diapers}:**
- 2-subset {Bread, Milk} $\notin L_2$ $\Rightarrow$ **Pruned** ✓

**Candidate {Butter, Milk, Diapers}:**
- 2-subset {Butter, Milk} $\in L_2$ ✓
- 2-subset {Butter, Diapers} $\in L_2$ ✓
- 2-subset {Milk, Diapers} $\in L_2$ ✓
- **Survives** pruning. Now scan database.

### Step 8: Count Support for {Butter, Milk, Diapers}

Examining each transaction:

| TID | Items | Contains B+M+D? | Count |
|-----|-------|-----------------|-------|
| T100 | {B, Bu, M, D} | ✓ | 1 |
| T200 | {B, Bu, D} | ✗ (no M) | — |
| T300 | {Bu, M} | ✗ (no D) | — |
| T400 | {B, M, D} | ✗ (no Bu) | — |
| T500 | {Bu, M, D} | ✓ | 2 |

Total $\sigma(\{Bu, M, D\}) = 2$, support = $2/5 = 40\% < 60\%$. Pruned.

### Step 9: Termination

$$L_3 = \emptyset \implies \text{Apriori terminates}$$

## 3.5 Generating Strong Association Rules from L₂

For each frequent 2-itemset, enumerate all non-empty proper subsets $X$ and form rules $X \Rightarrow Y = L_2 \setminus X$:

### Rule: {Bread, Diapers}

$$\text{Confidence}(\text{Bread} \Rightarrow \text{Diapers}) = \frac{\sigma(\{B, D\})}{\sigma(\{B\})} = \frac{3}{3} = 1.000 = 100\% \;\; \checkmark$$

$$\text{Confidence}(\text{Diapers} \Rightarrow \text{Bread}) = \frac{\sigma(\{B, D\})}{\sigma(\{D\})} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.750 = 75\% \;\; \checkmark$$

### Rule: {Butter, Milk}

$$\text{Confidence}(\text{Butter} \Rightarrow \text{Milk}) = \frac{\sigma(\{Bu, M\})}{\sigma(\{Bu\})} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.750 = 75\% \;\; \checkmark$$

$$\text{Confidence}(\text{Milk} \Rightarrow \text{Butter}) = \frac{\sigma(\{Bu, M\})}{\sigma(\{M\})} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.750 = 75\% \;\; \checkmark$$

### Rule: {Butter, Diapers}

$$\text{Confidence}(\text{Butter} \Rightarrow \text{Diapers}) = \frac{3}{4} = 75\% \;\; \checkmark$$

$$\text{Confidence}(\text{Diapers} \Rightarrow \text{Butter}) = \frac{3}{4} = 75\% \;\; \checkmark$$

### Rule: {Milk, Diapers}

$$\text{Confidence}(\text{Milk} \Rightarrow \text{Diapers}) = \frac{3}{4} = 75\% \;\; \checkmark$$

$$\text{Confidence}(\text{Diapers} \Rightarrow \text{Milk}) = \frac{3}{4} = 75\% \;\; \checkmark$$

## 3.6 Computing Lift for the Strongest Rule

For the rule *Bread ⇒ Diapers*:

$$\text{Lift}(\text{Bread} \Rightarrow \text{Diapers}) = \frac{s(\{B, D\})}{s(\{B\}) \cdot s(\{D\})} = \frac{3/5}{(3/5) \cdot (4/5)} = \frac{0.600}{0.480} = 1.250$$

Since $\text{Lift} = 1.25 > 1$, Bread and Diapers have a **positive correlation** (they co-occur 25% more often than independent chance).

## 3.7 Sequential Pattern Mining — GSP Algorithm Walkthrough

### Reference Sequence Database

| SID | Shopping Sequence |
|-----|-------------------|
| S100 | ⟨Bread, Butter, Milk, Diapers⟩ |
| S200 | ⟨Bread, Butter, Diapers⟩ |
| S300 | ⟨Butter, Milk⟩ |
| S400 | ⟨Bread, Milk, Diapers⟩ |
| S500 | ⟨Butter, Milk, Diapers⟩ |

**Parameter:** $\text{min\_sup} = 60\% \implies \text{min\_sup\_count} = 3$

### Stage 1: Frequent 1-Sequences (Single Items)

| 1-Sequence | Occurs in SIDs | Count | Frequent? |
|------------|----------------|-------|-----------|
| ⟨Bread⟩ | S100, S200, S400 | 3 | ✓ |
| ⟨Butter⟩ | S100, S200, S300, S500 | 4 | ✓ |
| ⟨Milk⟩ | S100, S300, S400, S500 | 4 | ✓ |
| ⟨Diapers⟩ | S100, S200, S400, S500 | 4 | ✓ |

### Stage 2: Candidate 2-Sequences and Support Count

| 2-Sequence | Support Count | Frequent? |
|------------|---------------|-----------|
| ⟨Bread, Butter⟩ | 2 (S100, S200) | ✗ |
| ⟨Bread, Milk⟩ | 2 (S100, S400) | ✗ |
| ⟨Bread, Diapers⟩ | 3 (S100, S200, S400) | ✓ |
| ⟨Butter, Milk⟩ | 3 (S100, S300, S500) | ✓ |
| ⟨Butter, Diapers⟩ | 3 (S100, S200, S500) | ✓ |
| ⟨Milk, Diapers⟩ | 3 (S100, S400, S500) | ✓ |

### Stage 3: Candidate 3-Sequences

All possible 3-element extensions — minimum support = 2 in each case → all pruned.

### Final Sequential Patterns

$$\mathcal{S} = \{\langle Bread, Diapers\rangle, \langle Butter, Milk\rangle, \langle Butter, Diapers\rangle, \langle Milk, Diapers\rangle\}$$

Each is a *temporally-ordered* pattern with support = 3.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Apriori Algorithm — Process Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start Transaction Database D]
    nodeB[Initialize k to 1]
    nodeC[Generate Candidate k-itemsets Ck via self-join Lk-1]
    nodeD[Apply Apriori Pruning Remove candidates with infrequent subsets]
    nodeE[Scan Database D to compute support of each Ck candidate]
    nodeF{Support of candidate greater than or equal to min_sup}
    nodeG[Add to Frequent Itemsets Lk]
    nodeH{Lk equals empty set}
    nodeI[Terminate Output Union of all Lk]
    nodeJ[Increment k by 1]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF -->|Yes| nodeG
    nodeF -->|No| nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH -->|Yes| nodeI
    nodeH -->|No| nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeC
```

## 4.2 FP-Growth — Two-Phase Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    phase1[PHASE 1: FP-Tree Construction]
    scan1[Database Scan 1 Count item frequencies]
    scan2[Database Scan 2 Build prefix tree and header table]
    tree[Compressed FP-Tree Structure]

    phase2[PHASE 2: Recursive Pattern Mining]
    mine[For each frequent item build conditional pattern base]
    condtree[Construct Conditional FP-Tree]
    recur[Recursively mine until single path]
    output[Output Frequent Patterns]

    scan1 --> scan2
    scan2 --> tree
    tree --> mine
    mine --> condtree
    condtree --> recur
    recur --> output
```

## 4.3 Sequential Rule Mining — GSP Algorithm Iteration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Sequence Database SDB]
    step2[Find Frequent 1-Sequences F1]
    step3[Set k equals 2]
    step4[Generate Candidate k-Sequences Ck by self-join and extension]
    step5[Prune using Apriori-like property for sequences]
    step6[Count support by checking containment in each customer sequence]
    step7{Minimum Support satisfied}
    step8[Add to Frequent Sequential Patterns Fk]
    step9{Fk empty}
    step10[Stop Output All Frequent Sequences]
    step11[Increment k]

    step1 --> step2
    step2 --> step3
    step3 --> step4
    step4 --> step5
    step5 --> step6
    step6 --> step7
    step7 -->|Yes| step8
    step7 -->|No| step9
    step8 --> step9
    step9 -->|Yes| step10
    step9 -->|No| step11
    step11 --> step4
```

## 4.4 Comparative Block Architecture — ARM vs. SRM

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ARM[ASSOCIATION RULE MINING]
        a1[Transactional Database]
        a2[Each transaction is an Unordered Set]
        a3[Output Frequent Itemsets and Rules]
        a4[Metrics Support Confidence Lift]
        a1 --> a2 --> a3
        a3 --> a4
    end

    subgraph SRM[SEQUENTIAL RULE MINING]
        s1[Sequence Database]
        s2[Each record is an Ordered List of Itemsets]
        s3[Output Frequent Subsequences]
        s4[Metric Support Only Plus Sequence Confidence]
        s1 --> s2 --> s3
        s3 --> s4
    end

    ARM -. adds temporal order .-> SRM
```

## 4.5 Pattern Evaluation Strategy — Block Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    raw[Raw Frequent Patterns from Algorithm]
    raw --> supf[Step 1 Apply Minimum Support Threshold]
    supf --> conff[Step 2 Apply Minimum Confidence Threshold]
    conff --> liftf[Step 3 Apply Lift Threshold greater than 1]
    liftf --> convf[Step 4 Optional Conviction Filter]
    convf --> final[Step 5 Output Actionable Rules]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**Define the terms *support*, *confidence*, and *lift* in association rule mining. Explain the significance of lift being greater than 1.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Support** of a rule $X \Rightarrow Y$ measures the fraction of transactions in the database containing both $X$ and $Y$:
>
> $$s(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{\sigma(X \cup Y)}{N}$$
>
> where $\sigma(X \cup Y)$ is the number of transactions containing $X \cup Y$ and $N$ is the total number of transactions.
>
> **Confidence** measures the conditional probability of $Y$ given $X$:
>
> $$c(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{s(X \cup Y)}{s(X)} = \frac{\sigma(X \cup Y)}{\sigma(X)}$$
>
> **Lift** quantifies how much the occurrence of $X$ *increases* the probability of $Y$ compared to $Y$'s baseline occurrence:
>
> $$\text{Lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{c(X \Rightarrow Y)}{s(Y)} = \frac{s(X \cup Y)}{s(X) \cdot s(Y)}$$
>
> When $\text{Lift} > 1$, items $X$ and $Y$ are **positively correlated** — they co-occur more often than statistical independence would predict, making the rule genuinely *interesting* rather than merely a consequence of $Y$ being generally popular. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**State and explain the Apriori property with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Apriori property** (also known as *anti-monotonicity* or the *downward closure property*) states:
>
> *"If an itemset $I$ is infrequent (i.e., has support less than min_sup), then any superset of $I$ is also infrequent."*
>
> Equivalently, **all non-empty subsets of a frequent itemset must also be frequent**.
>
> **Example:** Consider itemsets $\{A, B\}$ (support = 4) and $\{A, B, C\}$ (support = 2) with $\text{min\_sup} = 3$. The itemset $\{A, B, C\}$ is infrequent because its subset $\{A, B, C\}$... actually its subsets $\{A, C\}$ and $\{B, C\}$ may be infrequent. By Apriori property, since $\{A, B, C\}$ has support 2 < 3, we can immediately prune it without testing $\{A, B, C, D\}$, $\{A, B, C, E\}$, etc. This pruning drastically reduces the search space from $2^M$ candidates to a manageable subset. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

#### (a) Explain the Apriori algorithm for mining frequent itemsets. Describe each iteration with an example database of 5 transactions. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The **Apriori algorithm**, proposed by Agrawal and Srikant (1994), is a level-wise, breadth-first algorithm that uses the Apriori property for efficient pruning.

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Initialize** $k = 1$.
2. Generate the candidate $k$-itemsets $C_k$ via self-join of $L_{k-1}$.
3. **Prune** $C_k$ by removing any candidate whose $(k-1)$-subset is not in $L_{k-1}$.
4. **Scan** the database to compute the support count of each candidate in $C_k$.
5. **Filter** candidates whose support $\geq \text{min\_sup}$ to form $L_k$.
6. If $L_k \neq \emptyset$, increment $k$ and go to Step 2; else terminate.

**Example:** Using the database from Section 3.1 with $\text{min\_sup} = 60\%$ ($\text{min\_sup\_count} = 3$):

- **Iteration 1:** $L_1 = \{\{Bread\}, \{Butter\}, \{Milk\}, \{Diapers\}\}$
- **Iteration 2:** $C_2$ generated via self-join yields 6 candidates. After scanning, only 4 survive: $L_2 = \{\{B,D\}, \{Bu,M\}, \{Bu,D\}, \{M,D\}\}$
- **Iteration 3:** $C_3$ has 3 candidates. After Apriori pruning, only $\{Bu,M,D\}$ survives pruning — but its support (2) falls below 3, so it is discarded. $L_3 = \emptyset$, terminate.

**Complexity Analysis:** Apriori requires $k$ database scans (where $k$ is the size of the largest frequent itemset). The candidate generation at level $k$ can produce up to $\binom{\vert L_{k-1}\vert}{2}$ candidates.

#### (b) With a suitable example, explain sequential pattern mining. Discuss the GSP algorithm. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Sequential Pattern Mining** discovers frequently occurring ordered subsequences in a sequence database, where each record is an *ordered list* of itemsets (events).

**Difference from ARM:**

| Property | ARM | SRM |
|----------|-----|-----|
| Record structure | Unordered set | Ordered sequence |
| Output | Frequent itemsets | Frequent subsequences |
| Time dimension | Ignored | Explicit |

**GSP (Generalized Sequential Patterns) Algorithm:**

1. **Pass 1:** Scan database to find frequent 1-sequences $F_1$.
2. **Pass $k$:** Generate candidate $k$-sequences $C_k$ from $F_{k-1}$ using two operations:
   - **Contention:** Join two $(k-1)$-sequences with same $(k-2)$-prefix, appending the last item of second to the first.
   - **Extension:** Append a new item to either end of an existing sequence.
3. **Prune:** Remove candidates whose $(k-1)$-subsequences are not in $F_{k-1}$ (Apriori-like property for sequences).
4. **Count support** of each candidate and retain only those with support $\geq \text{min\_sup}$.

**Example:** From the sequence database in Section 3.7 with $\text{min\_sup} = 60\%$, the GSP algorithm discovers:

- $\langle Bread, Diapers \rangle$ (support = 3)
- $\langle Butter, Milk \rangle$ (support = 3)
- $\langle Butter, Diapers \rangle$ (support = 3)
- $\langle Milk, Diapers \rangle$ (support = 3)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert #1:** Students often confuse the *Apriori property for itemsets* (subsets of frequent itemset are frequent) with the *GSP Apriori property for sequences* (subsequences of a frequent sequence are frequent). Both are anti-monotone, but they operate on different mathematical objects — *sets* vs. *sequences*.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) Explain the FP-Growth algorithm. How does it overcome the limitations of Apriori? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The **FP-Growth (Frequent Pattern Growth)** algorithm, by Han et al. (2000), uses a divide-and-conquer strategy and a compact data structure called the **FP-Tree**.

**Two-Phase Process:**

**Phase 1 — FP-Tree Construction:**
1. Scan database once: compute frequency of each item, keep only frequent items (support $\geq \text{min\_sup}$).
2. Sort frequent items in *decreasing order of frequency*.
3. Scan database a second time: insert each transaction's frequent items (in sorted order) as a path in the tree, incrementing shared prefix counts and creating new nodes for divergences. Each node maintains: *item name*, *count*, *parent link*, *children links*, and a *node-link* pointing to the next occurrence of the same item.

**Phase 2 — Recursive Mining:**
1. For each frequent item (starting from least frequent), construct its **conditional pattern base** — the set of prefix paths leading to it.
2. Build a **conditional FP-Tree** from this base.
3. Recursively mine the conditional tree. A single-path tree yields all combinations of nodes on that path as frequent patterns.

**Overcoming Apriori's Limitations:**

| Limitation of Apriori | FP-Growth's Solution |
|----------------------|---------------------|
| Generates huge candidate sets | No candidate generation — pattern fragment growth only |
| Requires $k$ database scans | Only **2 scans** of the original database |
| Repeatedly scans and patterns DB | Tree compresses DB — fits in memory for dense datasets |
| Costly string operations on candidates | Uses compact pointer-based tree traversal |

**Drawback of FP-Growth:** FP-Tree construction can be expensive for sparse, high-dimensional data; recursive conditional tree construction may face exponential blowup in worst case.

#### (b) Discuss the various interestingness measures: Support, Confidence, Lift, and Conviction. Show how confidence alone can be misleading. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

A rule $X \Rightarrow Y$ is evaluated using four primary interestingness measures:

1. **Support** (statistical significance):

$$s(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{\sigma(X \cup Y)}{N}$$

Low support $\Rightarrow$ rare pattern $\Rightarrow$ unreliable statistic.

2. **Confidence** (rule strength):

$$c(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{\sigma(X \cup Y)}{\sigma(X)}$$

3. **Lift** (correlation strength):

$$\text{Lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{s(X \cup Y)}{s(X) \cdot s(Y)}$$

4. **Conviction** (directional implication):

$$\text{Conv}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{1 - s(Y)}{1 - c(X \Rightarrow Y)}$$

**Why Confidence Alone Can Be Misleading — A Counter-Example:**

Consider a database of 10,000 transactions where:
- $s(Tea) = 0.80$ (8000 transactions)
- $s(Bread) = 0.40$ (4000 transactions)
- $s(Tea \cap Bread) = 0.32$ (3200 transactions)

The confidence of *Tea ⇒ Bread* is:

$$c = \frac{0.32}{0.80} = 0.40 = 40\%$$

But the *prior probability* of buying Bread is also 40%. So:

$$\text{Lift} = \frac{0.32}{0.80 \cdot 0.40} = \frac{0.32}{0.32} = 1.0$$

This means Tea and Bread are **statistically independent** — buying Tea does *not* increase the likelihood of buying Bread, even though 40% of Tea buyers buy Bread. The high confidence is entirely an artifact of Bread being popular anyway. Lift correctly identifies this independence, whereas confidence hides it.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert #2:** Many KTU answer scripts compute only support and confidence and ignore lift. The 2024 KTU valuation key explicitly tests the ability to recognize that high-confidence rules can be **statistically trivial**. Always compute and report lift for any rule with confidence $>$ min\_conf. Loss of 2–3 marks is common here.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 3**

- **Association Rule Mining** discovers IF–THEN patterns in transactional data; formally $X \Rightarrow Y$ where $X \cap Y = \emptyset$.

- **Three Core Metrics**: Support (popularity), Confidence (reliability), Lift (correlation). Know their formulas by heart.

- **Lift > 1** = positive correlation; **Lift = 1** = independence; **Lift < 1** = negative correlation. *Always compute lift for full credit.*

- **Apriori Property**: All subsets of a frequent itemset are frequent. This is *anti-monotone* w.r.t. set inclusion and is the foundation of efficient candidate pruning.

- **Apriori Algorithm**: Level-wise candidate generation + Apriori pruning + database scan. **Drawback**: Multiple DB scans, exponential candidate generation.

- **FP-Growth**: Uses FP-Tree compression + recursive conditional pattern base mining. **Only 2 DB scans**, no candidate generation.
