- **Sequential Rule Mining**: Handles *ordered* sequences (temporal/order dimension matters). GSP algorithm is the sequential analog of Apriori.

- **Closed vs Maximal Itemsets**: Closed preserves support info (preferred in exams); Maximal only indicates no larger frequent superset.

- **Conviction formula** handles the edge case of perfect rules ($c = 1$) by yielding $\infty$.

- **15-Minute Exam Tip**: When a question asks to "mine frequent itemsets using Apriori," structure your answer as: (1) State algorithm, (2) Show $C_1, L_1$, (3) Self-join, (4) Apply Apriori pruning explicitly, (5) Show $C_2, L_2$, (6) Continue until $L_k = \emptyset$.

- **Common Mistake**: Writing $|X \cup Y|$ instead of $\sigma(X \cup Y)$ for support count — these are *cardinality of union as a set* vs. *frequency of co-occurrence in DB*, which are different. Use $\sigma(X \cup Y)$ for support count.

- **Sequential vs. Itemset Distinction**: Itemset = $\{A, B\}$ (unordered). Sequence = $\langle A, B \rangle$ (ordered). This distinction is **frequently tested**.

- **Default min_sup thresholds** in KTU problems typically range from 30%–60%; always convert to absolute count by multiplying with $N$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clustering Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT751 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Clustering Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Clustering** is an **unsupervised learning technique** in data analytics that involves grouping a set of data objects into multiple groups (called **clusters**) such that objects within the same cluster are highly similar to each other (high **intra-cluster similarity**), while objects belonging to different clusters are highly dissimilar (low **inter-cluster similarity**).

Formally, given a dataset $D = \{x_1, x_2, x_3, \ldots, x_n\}$ in a $d$-dimensional feature space, clustering partitions $D$ into $k$ clusters $C = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_k\}$ such that:

$$C_1 \cup C_2 \cup \ldots \cup C_k = D \quad \text{and} \quad C_i \cap C_j = \emptyset \;\; \forall \; i \neq j$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Clustering is a *Module 3* core competency under the course outcome **CO3**: *"Apply clustering and association techniques to discover patterns in unlabeled data."* Students are expected to demonstrate working knowledge of **K-Means**, **Hierarchical**, and **DBSCAN** algorithms, along with distance metrics and cluster validity indices.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a **massive, unorganized library** with thousands of books scattered on the floor. The librarian (the clustering algorithm) doesn't know any predefined categories. However, by looking at the books, the librarian notices that:
- **Mathematics books** have similar cover designs, similar weights, and similar page counts.
- **Novels** look different — they are thinner, have glossy covers, and varied authors.
- **Cookbooks** are bulky with colorful food images on the cover.

Without being told any labels, the librarian **groups** the books into sensible piles based on intrinsic similarity. That is exactly what clustering does — it discovers the natural "piles" inside unlabeled data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Clustering does **not** require labeled training data. It is *unsupervised* — the algorithm figures out the groups entirely from the geometric/statistical properties of the data.

---

## 1.3 Why Clustering Matters in Engineering & Data Analytics

| Application Domain | Use of Clustering |
|---|---|
| **Customer Segmentation** | Group customers by purchasing behavior for targeted marketing |
| **Anomaly Detection** | Identify outliers lying far from any cluster centroid |
| **Image Compression** | Reduce color palette via color clustering (e.g., K-Means on RGB) |
| **Document Organization** | Group news articles by topic using TF-IDF + clustering |
| **Bio-informatics** | Cluster gene expression data to identify co-regulated genes |
| **Wireless Sensor Networks** | Cluster sensor nodes to extend network lifetime |

---

## 1.4 Classification of Clustering Techniques (Overview)

Clustering methods are broadly classified into the following families:

1. **Partitioning Methods** (e.g., **K-Means**, **K-Medoids / PAM**)
2. **Hierarchical Methods** (e.g., **Agglomerative**, **Divisive**)
3. **Density-Based Methods** (e.g., **DBSCAN**, **OPTICS**)
4. **Grid-Based Methods** (e.g., **STING**, **CLIQUE**)
5. **Model-Based Methods** (e.g., **Gaussian Mixture Models / EM**)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 focus** is on Partitioning (K-Means), Hierarchical, and Density-based (DBSCAN) methods. The other families are mentioned only for contextual completeness and are not part of the direct assessment syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Distance Metrics — The Foundation of Similarity

Every clustering algorithm fundamentally relies on a **distance (or similarity) function** that quantifies "how close" two data points are. KTU frequently tests the formulas for these metrics in Part A and Part B.

### 2.1.1 Euclidean Distance ($L_2$ norm)

The straight-line distance between two points $x = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_d)$ and $y = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_d)$:

$$d(x, y) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d}(x_i - y_i)^2}$$

### 2.1.2 Manhattan Distance ($L_1$ norm)

The sum of absolute coordinate differences (also called "city-block" distance):

$$d(x, y) = \sum_{i=1}^{d} \vert x_i - y_i \vert$$

### 2.1.3 Minkowski Distance ($L_p$ norm)

A generalized form where $p$ is a positive integer:

$$d(x, y) = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{d} \vert x_i - y_i \vert^{p} \right)^{1/p}$$

- $p = 1 \Rightarrow$ Manhattan
- $p = 2 \Rightarrow$ Euclidean
- $p \to \infty \Rightarrow$ Chebyshev distance

### 2.1.4 Cosine Similarity (for directional / high-dimensional sparse data)

$$\text{cos\_sim}(x, y) = \frac{x \cdot y}{\Vert x \Vert \cdot \Vert y \Vert} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{d} x_i y_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} x_i^{2}} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} y_i^{2}}}$$

The corresponding distance is $d_{\text{cos}} = 1 - \text{cos\_sim}(x, y)$.

---

## 2.2 K-Means Clustering — Deep Analysis

**K-Means** is an iterative, centroid-based partitioning algorithm proposed by **MacQueen (1967)** and refined by **Lloyd (1957)**. The "K" denotes the pre-specified number of clusters.

### 2.2.1 Algorithm Intuition

- You pick $K$ initial "representative points" (centroids) in the feature space.
- Every data point is assigned to its nearest centroid (forming $K$ clusters).
- The centroid of each cluster is recomputed as the **mean** of all points assigned to it.
- Steps 2 and 3 repeat until centroids no longer move (convergence).

### 2.2.2 Objective Function — Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)

K-Means minimizes the following objective:

$$J(C) = \sum_{j=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^{2}$$

where:
- $K$ = number of clusters
- $C_j$ = set of points in cluster $j$
- $\mu_j$ = centroid (mean) of cluster $j$, given by $\mu_j = \dfrac{1}{\vert C_j \vert}\sum_{x_i \in C_j} x_i$
- $J(C)$ is also called **inertia** or **WCSS (Within-Cluster Sum of Squares)**

### 2.2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of K-Means

1. **Initialize**: Choose $K$ initial centroids (randomly, or via *K-Means++* smart seeding).
2. **Assignment Step**: For each data point $x_i$, assign it to the cluster with the closest centroid:
   $$\text{cluster}(x_i) = \arg\min_{j \in \{1,\ldots,K\}} \; \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^{2}$$
3. **Update Step**: Recompute each centroid as the mean of all points assigned to its cluster.
4. **Convergence Check**: If centroids have stabilized (change below threshold $\epsilon$) OR a maximum iteration count is reached, **stop**. Else go to Step 2.
5. **Output**: Final cluster labels and centroid coordinates.

### 2.2.4 The Elbow Method (Choosing K)

Plot WCSS $J(C)$ vs. number of clusters $K$. The optimal $K$ is at the "elbow" — the point where adding more clusters yields only a marginal decrease in WCSS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use Elbow Method?** K-Means requires the user to specify $K$ in advance. Without a validation method, choosing $K$ is guesswork. The Elbow method provides a principled (though heuristic) way to pick $K$.

### 2.2.5 Silhouette Score (Better Validation)

For a single point $x_i$ in cluster $C_j$, the silhouette coefficient is:

$$s(x_i) = \frac{b(x_i) - a(x_i)}{\max\{a(x_i), \, b(x_i)\}}$$

where:
- $a(x_i) = \dfrac{1}{\vert C_j \vert - 1} \sum_{x_k \in C_j, \, k \neq i} \Vert x_i - x_k \Vert$ (mean intra-cluster distance)
- $b(x_i) = \min_{m \neq j} \dfrac{1}{\vert C_m \vert} \sum_{x_k \in C_m} \Vert x_i - x_k \Vert$ (mean distance to nearest neighboring cluster)

The overall silhouette score is the mean of $s(x_i)$ over all points. Range: $[-1, +1]$. Values close to **+1** indicate well-formed clusters.

---

## 2.3 Hierarchical Clustering — Deep Analysis

Hierarchical clustering builds a **tree of clusters** called a **dendrogram**. Two approaches exist:

### 2.3.1 Agglomerative (Bottom-Up)

1. Start with $n$ singleton clusters (each point is its own cluster).
2. Repeatedly merge the two closest clusters.
3. Stop when all points are in a single cluster (or a desired number of clusters is reached).

### 2.3.2 Linkage Criteria (for measuring inter-cluster distance)

| Linkage | Formula | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Single Linkage** | $d(C_a, C_b) = \min_{x \in C_a, \, y \in C_b} d(x, y)$ | Tends to produce long, chain-like clusters |
| **Complete Linkage** | $d(C_a, C_b) = \max_{x \in C_a, \, y \in C_b} d(x, y)$ | Tends to produce compact, spherical clusters |
| **Average Linkage** | $d(C_a, C_b) = \dfrac{1}{\vert C_a \vert \vert C_b \vert}\sum_{x \in C_a, y \in C_b} d(x, y)$ | Compromise between single and complete |
| **Ward's Linkage** | Minimizes increase in WCSS when merging | Best for compact, equal-sized clusters |

### 2.3.3 Divisive (Top-Down)

The opposite of agglomerative: start with one cluster containing all points, and recursively split clusters until each point is alone. Less common in practice (e.g., **DIANA** algorithm).

> [!NOTE]
> **Dendrogram Interpretation:** The y-axis height at which two clusters merge represents the distance between them. Cut the dendrogram horizontally at a chosen height to obtain $K$ clusters.

---

## 2.4 DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise)

Unlike K-Means, **DBSCAN** does not require specifying $K$ in advance and can discover clusters of **arbitrary shape**. It also explicitly identifies **noise / outliers**.

### 2.4.1 Key Parameters

- **$\varepsilon$ (eps)**: Radius of the neighborhood around a point.
- **MinPts**: Minimum number of points required within $\varepsilon$ to form a dense region.

### 2.4.2 Point Classifications

A point $x$ is classified as one of three types:

1. **Core point**: Has at least **MinPts** neighbors within distance $\varepsilon$.
2. **Border point**: Has fewer than **MinPts** neighbors, but lies within $\varepsilon$ of a core point.
3. **Noise point**: Neither core nor border — labeled as **outlier**.

Formally, the $\varepsilon$-neighborhood of a point $p$ is:

$$N_{\varepsilon}(p) = \{q \in D \mid d(p, q) \leq \varepsilon\}$$

A point $p$ is a **core point** if $\vert N_{\varepsilon}(p) \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$.

### 2.4.3 Density-Reachability

- Point $q$ is **directly density-reachable** from $p$ if $q \in N_{\varepsilon}(p)$ and $p$ is a core point.
- Point $q$ is **density-reachable** from $p$ if there exists a chain of directly density-reachable points linking them.
- Points $p$ and $q$ are **density-connected** if there exists a point $o$ such that both $p$ and $q$ are density-reachable from $o$.

A **DBSCAN cluster** is a maximal set of density-connected points.

### 2.4.4 DBSCAN Algorithm

1. Pick an unvisited point $p$.
2. Retrieve its $\varepsilon$-neighborhood $N_{\varepsilon}(p)$.
3. If $\vert N_{\varepsilon}(p) \vert < \text{MinPts}$, mark $p$ as **noise** (temporarily) and move to next point.
4. Else, mark $p$ as a **core point**, start a new cluster, and recursively add all density-reachable points to it.
5. Repeat until all points are visited.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Use / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Euclidean distance | $d(x,y) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d}(x_i-y_i)^2}$ | Default metric in K-Means |
| 2 | Manhattan distance | $d(x,y) = \sum_{i=1}^{d} \vert x_i - y_i \vert$ | Robust to outliers; grid-based motion |
| 3 | Minkowski distance | $d(x,y) = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{d} \vert x_i - y_i \vert^{p}\right)^{1/p}$ | Generalization; $p \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| 4 | Cosine similarity | $\text{sim}(x,y) = \dfrac{x \cdot y}{\Vert x \Vert \Vert y \Vert}$ | Used for text/document data |
| 5 | Cosine distance | $d = 1 - \text{sim}(x,y)$ | Range $[0, 2]$ |
| 6 | K-Means centroid update | $\mu_j = \dfrac{1}{\vert C_j \vert}\sum_{x_i \in C_j} x_i$ | Mean of all points in cluster $j$ |
| 7 | K-Means objective (WCSS) | $J = \sum_{j=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^{2}$ | Minimized by K-Means iterations |
| 8 | Silhouette coefficient | $s(x_i) = \dfrac{b(x_i) - a(x_i)}{\max\{a(x_i), b(x_i)\}}$ | Validates $K$ and cluster quality |
| 9 | DBSCAN $\varepsilon$-neighborhood | $N_{\varepsilon}(p) = \{q \in D \mid d(p,q) \leq \varepsilon\}$ | Defines local density |
| 10 | Core point condition | $\vert N_{\varepsilon}(p) \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$ | Used to grow DBSCAN clusters |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Examination Tip:** When the question says "list the distance metrics", examiners expect the **formulas** along with the **special cases** (e.g., Minkowski for $p=1$ and $p=2$). Memorize the formula sheet above **verbatim** — these are the most frequently tested KTU questions.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Algorithm | Industry Application |
|---|---|
| **K-Means** | Customer segmentation in retail, vector quantization in image compression, load profiling in smart grids |
| **Hierarchical (Agglomerative)** | Taxonomy creation in biology (gene expression, phylogenetics), document hierarchy in NLP |
| **DBSCAN** | Geospatial anomaly detection, fraud detection in finance, identifying traffic hotspots in IoT/transport engineering |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: K-Means on a 2-D Toy Dataset

**Dataset (2-D points):**
$D = \{(2,10), (2,5), (8,4), (5,8), (7,5), (6,4), (1,2), (4,9)\}$
Let $K = 2$ and initial centroids be:
$\mu_1^{(0)} = (2, 10)$, $\mu_2^{(0)} = (5, 8)$.

### Iteration 1 — Assignment Step

Compute squared Euclidean distance from each point to both centroids, and assign to nearest.

| Point | $d^2$ to $\mu_1^{(0)}$ | $d^2$ to $\mu_2^{(0)}$ | Assigned to |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A=(2,10)$ | $0$ | $9$ | $C_1$ |
| $B=(2,5)$ | $25$ | $18$ | $C_2$ |
| $C=(8,4)$ | $72$ | $25$ | $C_2$ |
| $D=(5,8)$ | $9$ | $0$ | $C_2$ |
| $E=(7,5)$ | $50$ | $9$ | $C_2$ |
| $F=(6,4)$ | $52$ | $5$ | $C_2$ |
| $G=(1,2)$ | $65$ | $32$ | $C_2$ |
| $H=(4,9)$ | $5$ | $2$ | $C_2$ |

**Resulting clusters:**
$C_1 = \{(2,10)\}$, $C_2 = \{(2,5), (8,4), (5,8), (7,5), (6,4), (1,2), (4,9)\}$

### Iteration 1 — Update Step (Recompute Centroid Means)

$$\mu_1^{(1)} = \frac{1}{1} \sum_{x \in C_1} x = (2, 10)$$

For $C_2$ (7 points):

$$\mu_2^{(1)} = \left(\frac{2+8+5+7+6+1+4}{7}, \, \frac{5+4+8+5+4+2+9}{7}\right)$$

$$\mu_2^{(1)} = \left(\frac{33}{7}, \, \frac{37}{7}\right) \approx (4.71, \, 5.29)$$

### Iteration 2 — Assignment Step (with updated centroids)

Reassign all points to the closest of $\mu_1^{(1)} = (2,10)$ and $\mu_2^{(1)} = (4.71, 5.29)$:

| Point | $d^2$ to $\mu_1^{(1)}$ | $d^2$ to $\mu_2^{(1)}$ | Assigned to |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A=(2,10)$ | $0$ | $26.65$ | $C_1$ |
| $B=(2,5)$ | $25$ | $7.30$ | $C_2$ |
| $C=(8,4)$ | $72$ | $11.59$ | $C_2$ |
| $D=(5,8)$ | $9$ | $7.34$ | $C_2$ |
| $E=(7,5)$ | $50$ | $5.30$ | $C_2$ |
| $F=(6,4)$ | $52$ | $2.15$ | $C_2$ |
| $G=(1,2)$ | $65$ | $24.86$ | $C_2$ |
| $H=(4,9)$ | $5$ | $14.24$ | $C_2$ |

**Resulting clusters:** $C_1 = \{(2,10)\}$, $C_2 = \{(2,5), (8,4), (5,8), (7,5), (6,4), (1,2), (4,9)\}$

**Cluster assignments are unchanged**, so the algorithm has **converged**.

### Final Centroids

$$\mu_1 = (2, 10), \quad \mu_2 = \left(\frac{33}{7}, \frac{37}{7}\right) \approx (4.71, 5.29)$$

### Final WCSS (Objective Function Value)

Compute $J$ at convergence:

$$J = \sum_{j=1}^{2} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^{2}$$

For $C_1$ (one point): $J_1 = \Vert (2,10) - (2,10) \Vert^{2} = 0$.

For $C_2$ (seven points):
- $B = (2,5)$: $\Vert (2,5) - (4.71, 5.29) \Vert^{2} = (2.71)^{2} + (0.29)^{2} = 7.35 + 0.08 = 7.43$
- $C = (8,4)$: $(3.29)^{2} + (1.29)^{2} = 10.82 + 1.66 = 12.48$
- $D = (5,8)$: $(0.29)^{2} + (2.71)^{2} = 0.08 + 7.35 = 7.43$
- $E = (7,5)$: $(2.29)^{2} + (0.29)^{2} = 5.24 + 0.08 = 5.32$
- $F = (6,4)$: $(1.29)^{2} + (1.29)^{2} = 1.66 + 1.66 = 3.32$
- $G = (1,2)$: $(3.71)^{2} + (3.29)^{2} = 13.76 + 10.82 = 24.58$
- $H = (4,9)$: $(0.71)^{2} + (3.71)^{2} = 0.50 + 13.76 = 14.26$

$$J_2 = 7.43 + 12.48 + 7.43 + 5.32 + 3.32 + 24.58 + 14.26 = 74.82$$

$$\boxed{J_{\text{final}} = 0 + 74.82 = 74.82}$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Clustering Algorithms

| Criterion | K-Means | Hierarchical (Agglomerative) | DBSCAN |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cluster shape** | Spherical / convex | Any (depends on linkage) | Arbitrary |
| **Requires $K$ in advance?** | Yes | No (cut dendrogram) | No |
| **Handles noise** | Poor (assigns to nearest) | Poor | Yes (explicitly labels noise) |
| **Scalability to large data** | Good ($O(n \cdot K \cdot I)$) | Poor ($O(n^2)$ memory) | Good ($O(n \log n)$ with indexing) |
| **Sensitivity to outliers** | High (mean is not robust) | Medium (linkage-dependent) | Low |
| **Determinism** | Non-deterministic (init-dependent) | Deterministic | Deterministic |
| **Parameters** | $K$ | Linkage type, distance metric, cut height | $\varepsilon$, MinPts |
| **Best for** | Large, spherical clusters | Small datasets, taxonomy | Arbitrary shapes, noise-heavy data |
| **Time complexity** | $O(n \cdot K \cdot I \cdot d)$ | $O(n^2 \log n)$ (naive) | $O(n \log n)$ |

(Where $n$ = data points, $K$ = clusters, $I$ = iterations, $d$ = dimensions.)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Taxonomy of Clustering Techniques

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CLUSTERING TECHNIQUES] --> B[PARTITIONING]
    A --> C[HIERARCHICAL]
    A --> D[DENSITY BASED]
    A --> E[GRID BASED]
    A --> F[MODEL BASED]
    B --> B1[K MEANS]
    B --> B2[K MEDOIDS PAM]
    C --> C1[AGGLOMERATIVE]
    C --> C2[DIVISIVE]
    C1 --> C1A[SINGLE LINKAGE]
    C1 --> C1B[COMPLETE LINKAGE]
    C1 --> C1C[AVERAGE LINKAGE]
    C1 --> C1D[WARD LINKAGE]
    D --> D1[DBSCAN]
    D --> D2[OPTICS]
    E --> E1[STING]
    E --> E2[CLIQUE]
    F --> F1[GAUSSIAN MIXTURE]
    F --> F2[EXPECTATION MAXIMIZATION]
```

## 4.2 K-Means Algorithm Flow (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST1[START] --> ST2[INPUT DATASET D AND CHOOSE K]
    ST2 --> ST3[INITIALIZE K CENTROIDS USING K MEANS PLUS PLUS]
    ST3 --> ST4[ASSIGN EACH POINT TO NEAREST CENTROID]
    ST4 --> ST5[RECOMPUTE EACH CENTROID AS MEAN OF ITS CLUSTER]
    ST5 --> ST6{CENTROIDS STABLE OR MAX ITERATIONS REACHED}
    ST6 -->|NO| ST4
    ST6 -->|YES| ST7[OUTPUT FINAL CLUSTERS AND CENTROIDS]
    ST7 --> ST8[END]
```

## 4.3 DBSCAN Decision Flow (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DB1[START] --> DB2[INPUT DATASET D EPSILON AND MINPTS]
    DB2 --> DB3[PICK UNVISITED POINT P]
    DB3 --> DB4[FIND EPSILON NEIGHBORHOOD OF P]
    DB4 --> DB5{NEIGHBORHOOD SIZE GREATER EQUAL MINPTS}
    DB5 -->|NO| DB6[MARK P AS NOISE TEMPORARILY]
    DB5 -->|YES| DB7[CREATE NEW CLUSTER AND ADD P]
    DB7 --> DB8[ADD ALL DENSITY REACHABLE POINTS TO CLUSTER]
    DB8 --> DB9[REPEAT UNTIL NO MORE REACHABLE POINTS]
    DB9 --> DB10[ALL POINTS VISITED]
    DB6 --> DB10
    DB10 --> DB11[OUTPUT CLUSTERS AND NOISE LABELS]
    DB11 --> DB12[END]
```

## 4.4 Agglomerative Hierarchical Clustering with Dendrogram Concept

```mermaid
graph TD
    H1[INITIAL N SINGLETON CLUSTERS] --> H2[COMPUTE PAIRWISE DISTANCE MATRIX]
    H2 --> H3[IDENTIFY TWO CLOSEST CLUSTERS]
    H3 --> H4[MERGE THEM INTO A NEW CLUSTER]
    H4 --> H5[UPDATE DISTANCE MATRIX USING LINKAGE RULE]
    H5 --> H6{ONLY ONE CLUSTER LEFT}
    H6 -->|NO| H3
    H6 -->|YES| H7[BUILD DENDROGRAM FROM MERGE HISTORY]
    H7 --> H8[CUT DENDROGRAM AT CHOSEN HEIGHT TO GET K CLUSTERS]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams matter in KTU valuation:** When answering "Explain K-Means algorithm" or "How does DBSCAN work?", a **flowchart** in the answer sheet scores significantly higher than a purely textual description. Examiners reward visual logic that clearly shows the **decision** and **loop** points.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define clustering. Mention any two applications of clustering in data analytics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Clustering is an unsupervised machine learning technique that groups similar data objects into clusters such that intra-cluster similarity is high and inter-cluster similarity is low. It does not rely on pre-labeled training data and is primarily used for exploratory data analysis.

**Applications (1 mark — any two):**
1. **Customer segmentation** in retail for targeted marketing.
2. **Document clustering** to organize news articles by topic.
3. **Anomaly detection** in network security (outliers form their own small clusters or are labeled as noise).
4. **Image compression** using K-Means on RGB pixel values.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between supervised learning and unsupervised learning. Give one example algorithm from each category.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Supervised Learning | Unsupervised Learning |
|---|---|---|
| **Data labels** | Uses labeled data (input–output pairs) | Uses unlabeled data |
| **Goal** | Learn a mapping from input to output | Discover hidden structure / patterns |
| **Example algorithm** | Decision Tree, KNN, Linear Regression | **K-Means**, DBSCAN, Apriori |
| **Evaluation** | Accuracy, precision, recall, RMSE | Silhouette score, WCSS, visual inspection |

**Examples (1 mark):**
- Supervised: **K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)**
- Unsupervised: **K-Means Clustering**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Each Part B question has **internal choice** — students answer EITHER Option A OR Option B. Each sub-part is worth **7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**A. (a)** Explain the K-Means clustering algorithm with a neat flowchart. State its objective function. (7 marks)
**(b)** For the dataset $D = \{(1,1), (1,2), (2,1), (8,8), (9,9), (8,9)\}$ with initial centroids $\mu_1 = (1,1)$ and $\mu_2 = (8,8)$, perform **one complete iteration** of K-Means and report the new cluster assignments and updated centroids. Use Euclidean distance. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — A(a)

**K-Means Algorithm (5 marks):**

K-Means is a centroid-based partitioning clustering algorithm. It groups $n$ data points into $K$ pre-specified clusters by minimizing the Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS).

**Steps:**
1. Choose $K$ and initialize $K$ centroids (randomly or via K-Means++).
2. **Assignment step**: For each data point $x_i$, assign it to the cluster whose centroid is closest:
   $$\text{cluster}(x_i) = \arg\min_{j \in \{1,\ldots,K\}} \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^{2}$$
3. **Update step**: Recompute each centroid as the mean of all points currently in its cluster:
   $$\mu_j = \frac{1}{\vert C_j \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} x_i$$
4. Repeat steps 2–3 until centroids do not change or a maximum iteration limit is reached.

**Flowchart (1 mark):** See the diagram in Section 4.2.

**Objective Function (1 mark):**
$$J = \sum_{j=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^{2}$$

---

#### Model Solution — A(b)

**Step 1: Compute Squared Euclidean Distances in Iteration 1**

| Point | $d^2$ to $\mu_1=(1,1)$ | $d^2$ to $\mu_2=(8,8)$ | Assigned to |
|---|---|---|---|
| $(1,1)$ | $0$ | $98$ | $C_1$ |
| $(1,2)$ | $1$ | $85$ | $C_1$ |
| $(2,1)$ | $1$ | $85$ | $C_1$ |
| $(8,8)$ | $98$ | $0$ | $C_2$ |
| $(9,9)$ | $128$ | $2$ | $C_2$ |
| $(8,9)$ | $116$ | $1$ | $C_2$ |

**Step 2: New Cluster Assignments**
$C_1 = \{(1,1), (1,2), (2,1)\}$
$C_2 = \{(8,8), (9,9), (8,9)\}$

**Step 3: Update Centroids**

$$\mu_1^{\text{new}} = \left(\frac{1+1+2}{3}, \frac{1+2+1}{3}\right) = \left(\frac{4}{3}, \frac{4}{3}\right) \approx (1.33, 1.33)$$

$$\mu_2^{\text{new}} = \left(\frac{8+9+8}{3}, \frac{8+9+9}{3}\right) = \left(\frac{25}{3}, \frac{26}{3}\right) \approx (8.33, 8.67)$$

**Final Answer (Boxed):**

$$\boxed{\mu_1 = (1.33, 1.33), \quad \mu_2 = (8.33, 8.67)}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**B. (a)** Explain the DBSCAN algorithm. Define core, border, and noise points with the help of a diagram. (7 marks)
**(b)** For a 2-D dataset of 6 points with Euclidean distance, $\varepsilon = 1.5$ and MinPts = 3, identify the core, border, and noise points:
$P = \{(1,1), (1,2), (2,1), (8,8), (9,8), (8,9)\}$ (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — B(a)

**DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise)** is a density-based clustering algorithm that can discover clusters of **arbitrary shape** and explicitly label **outliers as noise**, without requiring the user to pre-specify $K$. (1 mark)

**Parameters:** $\varepsilon$ (neighborhood radius), MinPts (minimum number of points in a dense neighborhood). (1 mark)

**Point Classifications (4 marks):**

1. **Core point**: A point $p$ is a core point if its $\varepsilon$-neighborhood contains at least MinPts points:
   $$\vert N_{\varepsilon}(p) \vert = \vert\{q \in D \mid d(p,q) \leq \varepsilon\} \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$$
2. **Border point**: A point that is not a core point but lies within the $\varepsilon$-neighborhood of a core point.
3. **Noise point**: A point that is neither a core point nor a border point — labeled as an outlier.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to DBSCAN flowchart in Section 4.3.

---

#### Model Solution — B(b)

**Step 1: Compute $\varepsilon$-neighborhoods using Euclidean distance with $\varepsilon = 1.5$**

For point $A = (1,1)$:
- $d(A,B=(1,2)) = 1.0$ ✓
- $d(A,C=(2,1)) = 1.0$ ✓
- $d(A,D=(8,8)) = 9.90$ ✗
- $d(A,E=(9,8)) = 11.31$ ✗
- $d(A,F=(8,9)) = 11.40$ ✗

$N_{1.5}(A) = \{A, B, C\}$, so $\vert N_{1.5}(A) \vert = 3$.

**Step 2: Repeat for all points**

| Point | Neighbors within $\varepsilon$ | Count | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A=(1,1)$ | $\{A, B, C\}$ | 3 | **Core** (≥ MinPts=3) |
| $B=(1,2)$ | $\{A, B, C\}$ | 3 | **Core** |
| $C=(2,1)$ | $\{A, B, C\}$ | 3 | **Core** |
| $D=(8,8)$ | $\{D, E, F\}$ | 3 | **Core** |
| $E=(9,8)$ | $\{D, E, F\}$ | 3 | **Core** |
| $F=(8,9)$ | $\{D, E, F\}$ | 3 | **Core** |

**Step 3: Identify Border and Noise Points**

Since all points are core points, and there are two mutually distant dense groups (around (1,1) and (8,8)), there are no border or noise points.

**Step 4: Final Clusters**

$$C_1 = \{(1,1), (1,2), (2,1)\}, \quad C_2 = \{(8,8), (9,8), (8,9)\}$$

**Result:** 2 dense clusters, 0 noise points. (All 6 points are core points.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing K-Means with KNN**: K-Means is *unsupervised clustering*; KNN is *supervised classification*. Mixing them up costs full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to update centroids**: In K-Means iteration problems, students often correctly reassign points but forget to **recompute the centroid mean**. This is a 3-mark deduction in KTU pattern.
> 3. **Using $d$ instead of $d^2$ in K-Means**: K-Means uses squared Euclidean distance in its objective. Writing $d$ instead of $d^{2}$ in the minimization formula is a common error.
> 4. **DBSCAN Misclassification**: Calling every non-core point a "noise" point. Border points are different from noise — border points belong to a cluster; noise points do not.
> 5. **Hierarchical linkage confusion**: Single linkage uses **MIN**, complete linkage uses **MAX**, average linkage uses **MEAN**. Examiners frequently test this distinction.
> 6. **No flowchart in algorithm questions**: KTU's 2024 scheme explicitly rewards visual representations. Skipping a flowchart leads to 1–2 mark deductions on 7-mark sub-parts.

---

## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Clustering Techniques (Module 3)**

- [x] **Clustering** = unsupervised grouping of data into clusters of high intra-similarity and low inter-similarity.
- [x] **K-Means** requires pre-specifying $K$ and minimizes the **WCSS** objective $J = \sum_{j=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^{2}$.
- [x] K-Means steps: **(1)** Initialize centroids → **(2)** Assign each point to nearest centroid → **(3)** Recompute centroids as means → **(4)** Repeat until convergence.
- [x] **Elbow method** = plot WCSS vs. $K$ and pick the elbow point.
- [x] **Silhouette score** range: $[-1, +1]$; closer to $+1$ is better.
- [x] **Hierarchical clustering** produces a **dendrogram**. Agglomerative = bottom-up; Divisive = top-down.
- [x] Linkage types: **Single (MIN)**, **Complete (MAX)**, **Average (MEAN)**, **Ward's (WCSS-based)**.
- [x] **DBSCAN** parameters: $\varepsilon$ (radius) and **MinPts** (density threshold). Discovers arbitrary shapes; explicitly labels **noise**.
- [x] DBSCAN point types: **Core** ($\vert N_{\varepsilon}(p) \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$), **Border** (not core, but near a core), **Noise** (neither).
- [x] **Distance metrics**: Euclidean ($L_2$), Manhattan ($L_1$), Minkowski ($L_p$), Cosine similarity.
- [x] K-Means = sensitive to outliers & initialization (use **K-Means++** seeding for stability).
- [x] K-Means assumes **spherical, equal-sized** clusters; DBSCAN handles **arbitrary shapes** and noise better.
- [x] Time complexity: K-Means $\approx O(n \cdot K \cdot I \cdot d)$; Hierarchical $\approx O(n^2)$; DBSCAN $\approx O(n \log n)$.
- [x] **K-Medoids (PAM)** uses actual data points (medoids) as cluster centers, making it more **robust to outliers** than K-Means.
- [x] Always **standardize/normalize** features before applying distance-based clustering (z-score or min-max scaling).
- [x] Common validation indices: **WCSS**, **Silhouette**, **Davies–Bouldin Index (DBI)**, **Calinski–Harabasz Index**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT751 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hierarchical

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchical Clustering** is an unsupervised machine learning algorithm that builds a **nested tree of clusters**, called a **dendrogram**, by successively merging (agglomerative) or splitting (divisive) data points based on a chosen **distance/similarity metric** and **linkage criterion**.

In the KTU 2024 Data Analytics syllabus, hierarchical clustering is positioned as a **deterministic, non-iterative alternative to K-Means**, where the number of clusters $k$ is **not required a priori** — it is inferred by cutting the dendrogram at a chosen similarity threshold.

Mathematically, given a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$, hierarchical clustering produces a sequence of partitions:

$$P_0, P_1, P_2, \dots, P_{n-1}$$

where $P_0$ has each point as its own cluster, and $P_{n-1}$ has all points in one cluster, with each successive partition being a refinement/coarsening of the previous one.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Company Organizational Chart"**
>
> Imagine you walk into a multinational company and want to understand how employees are grouped:
> - **Top level** → Entire company (one big cluster)
> - **Next level** → Departments (Finance, HR, Engineering)
> - **Next level** → Teams inside each department
> - **Bottom level** → Individual employees
>
> **Hierarchical clustering works exactly like this!** It starts either:
> - **Bottom-up (Agglomerative):** Each employee is alone → groups with the closest colleague → forms teams → forms departments → forms the whole company. ✅
> - **Top-down (Divisive):** The whole company splits into departments → splits into teams → splits into individuals.

The **dendrogram** is the "organizational chart" that records *every level of grouping* simultaneously, letting the analyst pick any granularity they need.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Dendrogram** | A tree-like diagram that records the sequence of merges/splits and the distance at which each occurred. |
| **Linkage** | The rule that defines the distance between two clusters (not just two points). |
| **Cophenetic Distance** | The dendrogram-derived distance between two points — the height of the link at which they first merge. |
| **Agglomerative** | Bottom-up merging approach. |
| **Divisive** | Top-down splitting approach. |
| **Proximity Matrix** | An $n \times n$ matrix storing pairwise distances between all points/clusters. |

---

## 1.4 Why Hierarchical Clustering Matters in Data Analytics

> [!NOTE]
> Hierarchical clustering is **deterministic** (same input → same output every time) and produces a **full cluster hierarchy** rather than a flat partition. This makes it invaluable for:
> - **Taxonomy construction** (biology, marketing segmentation)
> - **Gene expression analysis** in bioinformatics
> - **Document clustering** for topic hierarchies
> - **Image segmentation** with region merging

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Variants of Hierarchical Clustering

### (A) Agglomerative (Bottom-Up) — *Most Common in KTU Exams*

1. Start with $n$ singleton clusters: $C_i = \{x_i\}$, for $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$.
2. Compute the $n \times n$ **proximity matrix** $D$.
3. **Repeat** until only one cluster remains:
   - Find the pair of clusters $(C_i, C_j)$ with the **smallest** inter-cluster distance.
   - **Merge** them into a new cluster $C_{ij} = C_i \cup C_j$.
   - **Update** the proximity matrix by computing distances from $C_{ij}$ to every other cluster using the chosen **linkage rule**.
4. Record each merge (and its distance) to build the dendrogram.

### (B) Divisive (Top-Down) — *Rare in exams, mentioned for completeness*

1. Start with one cluster containing all $n$ points.
2. At each step, select a cluster and **split** it into two sub-clusters (typically by running K-Means with $k=2$ on that cluster, or by using the largest intra-cluster distance).
3. Continue until each point is its own cluster.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Agglomerative is $O(n^3)$ time and $O(n^2)$ space in the naïve formulation, while Divisive is $O(2^n)$ in the worst case — making agglomerative the practical choice. KTU questions almost always assume **agglomerative**.

---

## 2.2 The Linkage Criteria — *The Heart of the Algorithm*

The linkage function $\mathcal{L}(C_a, C_b)$ defines how we measure the distance between two clusters. Let $d(x_i, x_j)$ denote the Euclidean (or any) distance between two points.

| Linkage | Formula | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Single Linkage** | $\mathcal{L}_{\min} = \min_{x_i \in C_a,\, x_j \in C_b} d(x_i, x_j)$ | Tends to produce **long, chain-like** clusters. Sensitive to noise. |
| **Complete Linkage** | $\mathcal{L}_{\max} = \max_{x_i \in C_a,\, x_j \in C_b} d(x_i, x_j)$ | Tends to produce **compact, spherical** clusters. Sensitive to outliers. |
| **Average Linkage** | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{1}{\vert C_a \vert \cdot \vert C_b \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_a}\sum_{x_j \in C_b} d(x_i, x_j)$ | Compromise between single and complete. **Most recommended.** |
| **Ward's Linkage** | $\Delta(C_a, C_b) = \dfrac{\vert C_a \vert \cdot \vert C_b \vert}{\vert C_a \vert + \vert C_b \vert} \, \big\Vert m_{ab} - m_a \big\Vert^2$ | Minimizes the **increase in total within-cluster variance**. Best for spherical, equal-size clusters. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Ward's method** is technically a linkage rule, but it is defined in terms of the **merge cost (increase in SSE)** rather than a simple pairwise distance. It is the default in most professional libraries (e.g., `scipy.cluster.hierarchy`).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D^{(0)} = [d(x_i, x_j)]_{n \times n}$ | Initial proximity matrix |
| 2 | $\mathcal{L}_{\min}(C_a, C_b) = \min d(x_i, x_j)$ | Single linkage |
| 3 | $\mathcal{L}_{\max}(C_a, C_b) = \max d(x_i, x_j)$ | Complete linkage |
| 4 | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{avg}}(C_a, C_b) = \tfrac{1}{\vert C_a \vert \cdot \vert C_b \vert} \sum\sum d$ | Average linkage |
| 5 | $\Delta_{\text{Ward}} = \frac{\vert C_a \vert \cdot \vert C_b \vert}{\vert C_a \vert + \vert C_b \vert} \, \Vert m_{ab} - m_a \Vert^2$ | Ward's merge cost |
| 6 | $c = \dfrac{\sum_{i<j} d(x_i, x_j) \cdot t(x_i, x_j)}{\sum_{i<j} d(x_i, x_j)^2}$ | **Cophenetic Correlation Coefficient** (CCC) — quality metric |
| 7 | $\text{SSE} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x \in C_k} \Vert x - \mu_k \Vert^2$ | Total within-cluster SSE |
| 8 | $h_{\text{merge}}(C_a, C_b) = \mathcal{L}(C_a, C_b)$ | Height of the dendrogram link |
| 9 | $k^\ast = \arg\max_{k} \, \text{silhouette}(k)$ | Choosing number of clusters |

> **Symbols used:** $\mu_k$ = centroid of cluster $C_k$, $\vert C \vert$ = cardinality, $m_{ab}$ = centroid of merged cluster, $t(x_i, x_j)$ = cophenetic distance.

---

## 2.4 The Lance–Williams Update Formula — *High-Yield KTU Topic*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> After merging $C_a$ and $C_b$ into $C_{ab}$, the distance to any other cluster $C_c$ can be updated **recursively** without recomputing from scratch:

$$d(C_{ab}, C_c) = \alpha_a \, d(C_a, C_c) + \alpha_b \, d(C_b, C_c) + \beta \, d(C_a, C_b) + \gamma \, \big\vert d(C_a, C_c) - d(C_b, C_c) \big\vert$$

| Linkage | $\alpha_a = \alpha_b$ | $\beta$ | $\gamma$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single | $1/2$ | $0$ | $-1/2$ |
| Complete | $1/2$ | $0$ | $+1/2$ |
| Average | $\frac{\vert C_a \vert}{\vert C_a \vert + \vert C_b \vert},\ \frac{\vert C_b \vert}{\vert C_a \vert + \vert C_b \vert}$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| Ward | $\frac{\vert C_a \vert + \vert C_c \vert}{\vert C_{ab} \vert + \vert C_c \vert}$ (symmetric) | $\frac{-\vert C_c \vert}{\vert C_{ab} \vert + \vert C_c \vert}$ | $0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most-tested formula in KTU university exams** under hierarchical clustering.

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Agglomerative Clustering by Hand

### Dataset

Consider 5 one-dimensional points: $\mathcal{D} = \{2, 4, 6, 8, 10\}$.

We will use **Euclidean distance** and **Single Linkage**.

---

### Step 1 — Initial Proximity Matrix

Compute all pairwise Euclidean distances $d(x_i, x_j) = \vert x_i - x_j \vert$.

The matrix $D^{(0)}$ is:

$$
D^{(0)} = \begin{array}{c|ccccc}
 & 2 & 4 & 6 & 8 & 10 \\
\hline
2  & 0 & 2 & 4 & 6 & 8 \\
4  & 2 & 0 & 2 & 4 & 6 \\
6  & 4 & 2 & 0 & 2 & 4 \\
8  & 6 & 4 & 2 & 0 & 2 \\
10 & 8 & 6 & 4 & 2 & 0
\end{array}
$$

> **Reasoning:** Each entry is just the absolute difference along the 1-D line. The matrix is symmetric with zeros on the diagonal.

---

### Step 2 — First Merge (Iteration 1)

Find the **smallest** non-zero entry in $D^{(0)}$.

The minimum is $d(2, 4) = d(4, 6) = d(6, 8) = d(8, 10) = 2$ (ties). Conventionally we pick the **first** minimum encountered, so we merge **$C_1 = \{2\}$ and $C_2 = \{4\}$** into $C_{12} = \{2, 4\}$ at height $h = 2$.

> **Reasoning:** With **single linkage**, the distance between any two clusters is the *minimum* pairwise distance. So $d(\{2\}, \{4\}) = 2$ is the smallest in the matrix.

---

### Step 3 — Update Proximity Matrix (Iteration 1 → 2)

Using **Single Linkage**:

$$d(C_{12}, C_3) = \min(d(2,6), d(4,6)) = \min(4, 2) = 2$$
$$d(C_{12}, C_4) = \min(d(2,8), d(4,8)) = \min(6, 4) = 4$$
$$d(C_{12}, C_5) = \min(d(2,10), d(4,10)) = \min(8, 6) = 6$$

> **Reasoning:** The single linkage update keeps only the closest pair across the two clusters. The Lance–Williams coefficients ($\alpha = 1/2, \beta = 0, \gamma = -1/2$) yield the same result.

The new matrix is:

$$
D^{(1)} = \begin{array}{c|cccc}
 & \{2,4\} & 6 & 8 & 10 \\
\hline
\{2,4\} & 0 & 2 & 4 & 6 \\
6       & 2 & 0 & 2 & 4 \\
8       & 4 & 2 & 0 & 2 \\
10      & 6 & 4 & 2 & 0
\end{array}
$$

---

### Step 4 — Iteration 2: Merge $C_{12}$ and $C_3 = \{6\}$

The minimum of $D^{(1)}$ is $d(\{2,4\}, 6) = 2$. Merge them.

$$C_{123} = \{2, 4, 6\}, \quad h = 2$$

Update distances from $C_{123}$:

$$d(C_{123}, 8) = \min(6, 4, 2) = 2, \quad d(C_{123}, 10) = \min(8, 6, 4) = 4$$

$$
D^{(2)} = \begin{array}{c|ccc}
 & \{2,4,6\} & 8 & 10 \\
\hline
\{2,4,6\} & 0 & 2 & 4 \\
8         & 2 & 0 & 2 \\
10        & 4 & 2 & 0
\end{array}
$$

---

### Step 5 — Iteration 3: Merge $\{2,4,6\}$ and $\{8\}$

Minimum is $2$. Merge at $h = 2$.

$$C_{1234} = \{2, 4, 6, 8\}, \quad d(C_{1234}, 10) = \min(8, 6, 4, 2) = 2$$

---

### Step 6 — Iteration 4: Final Merge

$$C_{12345} = \{2, 4, 6, 8, 10\}, \quad h = 2$$

> **Observation:** Every merge happened at the **same height** $h = 2$ because the points are equally spaced. This is the **uniform-spacing pathological case** for single linkage.

---

## 3.2 Complete Linkage — Same Dataset, Different Result

Using **Complete Linkage** on the same dataset:

$$d(C_a, C_b) = \max_{x_i \in C_a, x_j \in C_b} \vert x_i - x_j \vert$$

**Step 1:** Merge $\{2, 4\}$ (distance 2).

**Step 2:** $d(\{2,4\}, 6) = \max(4, 2) = 4$ — this is **larger** than the in-progress entries between singletons. So the next minimum is still between $\{6\}$ and $\{8\}$, merge them.

**Final Dendrogram Heights (Complete):**
- $h(\{2\}, \{4\}) = 2$
- $h(\{6\}, \{8\}) = 2$
- $h(\{2,4\}, \{6,8\}) = 6$
- $h(\{2,4,6,8\}, \{10\}) = 8$

> **Key insight:** Single linkage produces a "chained" dendrogram (all heights equal at 2), while complete linkage separates the natural group $\{2,4,6,8\}$ from the outlier $\{10\}$ at a much greater height.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Ward's Linkage Cost

We minimize the increase in total within-cluster sum of squares (SSE).

Let $C_a, C_b$ have sizes $n_a, n_b$ and centroids $m_a, m_b$. After merging into $C_{ab}$:

$$m_{ab} = \frac{n_a \, m_a + n_b \, m_b}{n_a + n_b}$$

The increase in SSE is:

$$
\Delta(C_a, C_b) = \sum_{x \in C_{ab}} \Vert x - m_{ab} \Vert^2 - \sum_{x \in C_a} \Vert x - m_a \Vert^2 - \sum_{x \in C_b} \Vert x - m_b \Vert^2
$$

After algebraic simplification (using the parallel-axis theorem):

$$
\Delta(C_a, C_b) = \frac{n_a \cdot n_b}{n_a + n_b} \, \Vert m_a - m_b \Vert^2
$$

> **Reasoning:** The numerator $n_a n_b$ weighs the merge by cluster size; the denominator $n_a + n_b$ normalizes. This is the **exact** formula used in scipy's `Ward` linkage.

---

## 3.4 Cophenetic Correlation Coefficient (CCC) — Quality of Dendrogram

> [!IMPORTANT]
> CCC measures **how faithfully** the dendrogram preserves the original pairwise distances. CCC $\in [-1, 1]$, and **values close to 1** indicate a good hierarchical structure.

$$
c = \frac{\sum_{i<j} d(x_i, x_j) \cdot t(x_i, x_j)}{\sqrt{\left(\sum_{i<j} d(x_i, x_j)^2\right) \left(\sum_{i<j} t(x_i, x_j)^2\right)}
$$

where $t(x_i, x_j)$ is the **cophenetic distance** — the dendrogram height at which $x_i$ and $x_j$ are first joined.

> **Use case:** When you cannot decide between single, complete, average, and Ward linkage, compute the CCC for each and **pick the linkage with the highest CCC**.

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Agglomerative Clustering — Full Block-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Dataset D with n points] --> B[Compute n x n Proximity Matrix D0]
    B --> C{Is there only 1 cluster left?}
    C -- No --> D[Find pair of clusters with MIN distance d_min]
    D --> E[Merge them into a new cluster C_new]
    E --> F[Update Proximity Matrix using Lance-Williams formula]
    F --> G[Record merge event: cluster IDs + height h = d_min]
    G --> H[Store in dendrogram log]
    H --> C
    C -- Yes --> I[Build the full dendrogram from merge log]
    I --> J[Apply cut at user-chosen threshold epsilon to get k clusters]
    J --> K[Output: Final cluster labels for all n points]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The loop on the right (C → D → E → F → G → H → C) runs exactly $n - 1$ times. Each iteration merges two clusters and updates the proximity matrix, until only one cluster remains.

---

## 4.2 Dendrogram Structure for the Worked Example

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1["h = 2 : {2,4,6,8,10}"] --> L2A["h = 2 : {2,4,6,8}"]
    L1 --> L2B["h = 2 : {10}"]
    L2A --> L3A["h = 2 : {2,4,6}"]
    L2A --> L3B["h = 2 : {8}"]
    L2B --> L3C["h = 2 : {10} (singleton)"]
    L3A --> L4A["h = 2 : {2,4}"]
    L3A --> L4B["h = 2 : {6}"]
    L4A --> L5A["h = 2 : {2}"]
    L4A --> L5B["h = 2 : {4}"]
```

> **Note:** All merges happened at $h = 2$ because the points are equally spaced. In a real-world dataset, the heights would increase as clusters become more dissimilar.

---

## 4.3 Decision Map — Choosing the Right Linkage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: What is your data shape?] --> Q1{Is the dataset NOISY?}
    Q1 -- Yes, lots of noise --> R1[Use COMPLETE or AVERAGE linkage]
    Q1 -- No, relatively clean --> Q2{Do you want to detect ELONGATED or chain-shaped clusters?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R2[Use SINGLE linkage]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Do you want COMPACT, EQUAL-SIZE spherical clusters?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> R3[Use WARD linkage]
    Q3 -- No, general purpose --> R4[Use AVERAGE linkage with CCC validation]
```

> **Engineering Tip:** In KTU practical exams and industry projects, **Ward linkage + Euclidean distance** is the safest default. Use single linkage only for non-convex (snake-like) clusters.

---

## 4.4 Time & Space Complexity Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Agglomerative Hierarchical Clustering] --> B[Time: O n cubed naive]
    A --> C[Space: O n squared]
    A --> D[Output: Full dendrogram]
    D --> E[Cut dendrogram at any threshold to get any k]
    B --> F[Infeasible for n > 10,000]
    C --> G[Use BIRCH or CLINK for large n]
```
