# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🔹 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define hierarchical clustering. Differentiate between agglomerative and divisive hierarchical clustering.

**Model Answer:**

> **Hierarchical clustering** is an unsupervised learning technique that builds a hierarchy of clusters represented as a **dendrogram**, without requiring the number of clusters $k$ to be specified in advance.

| Feature | Agglomerative | Divisive |
|---|---|---|
| **Approach** | Bottom-up | Top-down |
| **Start state** | $n$ singleton clusters | 1 cluster with all $n$ points |
| **End state** | 1 cluster with all $n$ points | $n$ singleton clusters |
| **Operation** | Repeatedly **merge** the closest pair | Repeatedly **split** the most heterogeneous cluster |
| **Complexity** | $O(n^3)$ time, $O(n^2)$ space | $O(2^n)$ worst case |
| **Used in practice** | ✅ Yes — standard choice | ❌ Rare — exponential cost |

*(3 marks — full marks for definition + a clear 5-row contrast table.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is a dendrogram? Explain how the number of clusters is decided from it.

**Model Answer:**

A **dendrogram** is a tree-like diagram that records the **sequence of merges** (agglomerative) or **splits** (divisive) in hierarchical clustering. The **vertical axis** represents the **distance/dissimilarity** at which clusters are joined, and the **horizontal axis** represents the data points or sub-clusters.

**Choosing $k$:**

1. Draw a **horizontal cut** across the dendrogram at some height $h^\ast$.
2. The number of **vertical lines** the cut intersects = number of clusters $k$.
3. The optimal cut is usually at the **largest vertical gap** between consecutive merge heights — this indicates the most natural separation.

> The "largest gap" rule is a visual analog of the elbow method.

*(3 marks — 1.5 for definition, 1.5 for the cut procedure.)*

---

## 🔹 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### ❓ **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Agglomerative Algorithm + Lance–Williams*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

> **(a)** Explain the **Agglomerative Hierarchical Clustering** algorithm with a neat flowchart. Discuss any **two** linkage methods with formulas. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Consider the following 5 data points in 2-D space:
>
> $A(1, 1),\ B(1, 2),\ C(2, 1),\ D(6, 6),\ E(7, 7)$
>
> Apply **Agglomerative clustering with Complete Linkage** (Euclidean distance). Show the **proximity matrix at every step** and draw the **dendrogram**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. Initialize: Each point $x_i$ is a singleton cluster. Compute the $n \times n$ **proximity matrix** $D^{(0)}$.
2. **Loop** until one cluster remains:
   - Scan $D^{(t)}$ for the **smallest** distance $d_{\min}(C_i, C_j)$.
   - **Merge** $C_i, C_j \to C_{ij}$ at dendrogram height $h = d_{\min}$.
   - **Update** $D^{(t+1)}$ using the chosen **linkage rule**.
3. Stop when only one cluster is left.
4. Cut the dendrogram at the desired level to obtain $k$ clusters.

**Two Linkage Methods:**

**(i) Single Linkage:**
$$\mathcal{L}_{\min}(C_a, C_b) = \min_{x \in C_a, y \in C_b} d(x, y)$$

> Tendency: forms **chained, elongated** clusters; sensitive to noise and outliers.

**(ii) Complete Linkage:**
$$\mathcal{L}_{\max}(C_a, C_b) = \max_{x \in C_a, y \in C_b} d(x, y)$$

> Tendency: forms **compact, tight** clusters; outlier-sensitive.

*(7 marks: 3 for algorithm, 2 for linkage 1, 2 for linkage 2.)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute Euclidean Distances**

$$d(A,B) = \sqrt{(1-1)^2 + (1-2)^2} = 1$$
$$d(A,C) = \sqrt{(1-2)^2 + (1-1)^2} = 1$$
$$d(A,D) = \sqrt{(1-6)^2 + (1-6)^2} = \sqrt{50} \approx 7.07$$
$$d(A,E) = \sqrt{1+36} = \sqrt{37} \approx 6.08$$
$$d(B,C) = \sqrt{1+1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$$
$$d(B,D) = \sqrt{25+16} = \sqrt{41} \approx 6.40$$
$$d(B,E) = \sqrt{36+25} = \sqrt{61} \approx 7.81$$
$$d(C,D) = \sqrt{16+25} = \sqrt{41} \approx 6.40$$
$$d(C,E) = \sqrt{25+36} = \sqrt{61} \approx 7.81$$
$$d(D,E) = \sqrt{1+1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$$

> **Reasoning:** $A,B,C$ are clustered near $(1,1)$; $D,E$ are clustered near $(6.5, 6.5)$. We expect two natural groups.

**Initial Proximity Matrix $D^{(0)}$:**

$$
D^{(0)} = \begin{array}{c|ccccc}
 & A & B & C & D & E \\
\hline
A & 0 & 1.00 & 1.00 & 7.07 & 6.08 \\
B & 1.00 & 0 & 1.41 & 6.40 & 7.81 \\
C & 1.00 & 1.41 & 0 & 6.40 & 7.81 \\
D & 7.07 & 6.40 & 6.40 & 0 & 1.41 \\
E & 6.08 & 7.81 & 7.81 & 1.41 & 0
\end{array}
$$

**Step 2 — First Merge**

Minimum is $d(A, B) = 1.00$. Merge into $C_{AB} = \{A, B\}$ at height $h = 1.00$.

> **Reasoning:** Tie-breaking: $d(A, B) = d(A, C) = 1.00$. Conventionally we pick the lexicographically first pair. We take $(A, B)$.

**Step 3 — Update Matrix Using Complete Linkage**

$$d(C_{AB}, C) = \max(d(A, C), d(B, C)) = \max(1.00, 1.41) = 1.41$$
$$d(C_{AB}, D) = \max(d(A, D), d(B, D)) = \max(7.07, 6.40) = 7.07$$
$$d(C_{AB}, E) = \max(d(A, E), d(B, E)) = \max(6.08, 7.81) = 7.81$$
$$d(C, D) = 6.40, \quad d(C, E) = 7.81, \quad d(D, E) = 1.41$$

$$
D^{(1)} = \begin{array}{c|cccc}
 & \{A,B\} & C & D & E \\
\hline
\{A,B\} & 0 & 1.41 & 7.07 & 7.81 \\
C       & 1.41 & 0 & 6.40 & 7.81 \\
D       & 7.07 & 6.40 & 0 & 1.41 \\
E       & 7.81 & 7.81 & 1.41 & 0
\end{array}
$$

**Step 4 — Second Merge**

Minimum is $d(\{A,B\}, C) = 1.41$. Merge into $C_{ABC} = \{A, B, C\}$ at height $h = 1.41$.

$$d(C_{ABC}, D) = \max(d(A,D), d(B,D), d(C,D)) = \max(7.07, 6.40, 6.40) = 7.07$$
$$d(C_{ABC}, E) = \max(6.08, 7.81, 7.81) = 7.81$$
$$d(D, E) = 1.41$$

$$
D^{(2)} = \begin{array}{c|ccc}
 & \{A,B,C\} & D & E \\
\hline
\{A,B,C\} & 0 & 7.07 & 7.81 \\
D         & 7.07 & 0 & 1.41 \\
E         & 7.81 & 1.41 & 0
\end{array}
$$

**Step 5 — Third Merge**

Minimum is $d(D, E) = 1.41$. Merge into $C_{DE} = \{D, E\}$ at height $h = 1.41$.

$$d(C_{ABC}, C_{DE}) = \max(7.07, 7.81, 6.40, 6.40, 7.07, 7.81) = 7.81$$

$$
D^{(3)} = \begin{array}{c|cc}
 & \{A,B,C\} & \{D,E\} \\
\hline
\{A,B,C\} & 0 & 7.81 \\
\{D,E\}   & 7.81 & 0
\end{array}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Merge at $h = 7.81$**

All points joined.

**Dendrogram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOP["h = 7.81 : ALL POINTS"] --> M1["h = 1.41 : ABC"]
    TOP --> M2["h = 1.41 : DE"]
    M1 --> N1["h = 1.00 : AB"]
    M1 --> N2["h = 0 : C"]
    M2 --> N3["h = 0 : D"]
    M2 --> N4["h = 0 : E"]
    N1 --> P1["h = 0 : A"]
    N1 --> P2["h = 0 : B"]
```

> **Conclusion:** The largest vertical gap is between $h = 1.41$ and $h = 7.81$. Cutting here yields **$k = 2$ clusters**: $\{A, B, C\}$ and $\{D, E\}$. ✅

*(7 marks: 2 for distance calc, 2 for matrix updates, 2 for dendrogram, 1 for final $k$.)*

---

### ❓ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative: Linkage Comparison + Dendrogram Interpretation*

> **(a)** Compare **Single, Complete, Average, and Ward's** linkage methods in hierarchical clustering. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** The following dendrogram was obtained from a customer-segmentation study on 6 customers:
>
> - $\{C_1, C_2\}$ merge at height $2$
> - $\{C_3, C_4\}$ merge at height $3$
> - $\{C_1, C_2\} \cup \{C_3, C_4\}$ merge at height $8$
> - $C_5$ joins the big cluster at height $10$
> - $C_6$ joins the big cluster at height $14$
>
> **(i)** Draw the dendrogram.
> **(ii)** If the business wants exactly **3 segments**, identify the clusters.
> **(iii)** What is the cophenetic distance between $C_1$ and $C_6$? Between $C_5$ and $C_6$? **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

| Linkage | Formula | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Single** | $\min d(x,y)$ | Detects **non-convex / elongated** clusters | Forms **chaining**; sensitive to noise |
| **Complete** | $\max d(x,y)$ | Produces **compact, equal-diameter** clusters | Sensitive to **outliers**; bias toward spherical shapes |
| **Average** | $\text{mean of } d(x,y)$ | **Balanced** between single and complete | Computationally more expensive |
| **Ward's** | $\Delta = \frac{n_a n_b}{n_a + n_b} \Vert m_a - m_b \Vert^2$ | **Minimizes within-cluster SSE**; best for spherical clusters | Only works well with **Euclidean** distance; biased toward equal-size clusters |

*(7 marks — 1 mark per cell of advantage/disadvantage, plus 3 marks for the formulas and overall justification.)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Dendrogram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["h = 14 : ALL SIX CUSTOMERS"] --> A["h = 10 : {C1,C2,C3,C4,C5}"]
    R --> B["h = 0 : C6 (singleton)"]
    A --> A1["h = 8 : {C1,C2,C3,C4}"]
    A --> A2["h = 0 : C5 (singleton)"]
    A1 --> A1A["h = 2 : {C1,C2}"]
    A1 --> A1B["h = 3 : {C3,C4}"]
    A1A --> X1["C1"]
    A1A --> X2["C2"]
    A1B --> X3["C3"]
    A1B --> X4["C4"]
```

**(ii) Three Segments (cut at $h = 8$):**

$$C_1, C_2, C_3, C_4 \quad ; \quad C_5 \quad ; \quad C_6$$

> **Reasoning:** Cutting horizontally at $h = 8$ (the largest gap before the cluster inflates) gives **3 vertical lines**, hence **3 segments**: the tight group $\{C_1, C_2, C_3, C_4\}$, the loose member $C_5$, and the outlier $C_6$.

**(iii) Cophenetic Distances:**

- $C_1$ and $C_6$ first meet at the **root of the dendrogram**, height $h = 14$. So $t(C_1, C_6) = 14$.
- $C_5$ and $C_6$ also first meet at the root, height $h = 14$. So $t(C_5, C_6) = 14$.

> **Interpretation:** Both pairs are maximally dissimilar according to the dendrogram — they are the "last" members to join the cluster.

*(7 marks: 2 for dendrogram drawing, 2 for 3 segments, 3 for cophenetic distances.)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Hierarchical Clustering Questions:**
>
> 1. **Using min/max/mean wrongly with linkage:** Students often write *"average of all points in the cluster"* for **centroid linkage** and confuse it with **average linkage**. They are NOT the same! Centroid linkage uses $\Vert m_a - m_b \Vert$, average linkage uses $\frac{1}{n_a n_b}\sum\sum d(x_i, y_j)$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the update rule:** After merging two clusters, the proximity matrix **must be reduced by one row and one column**. Marks are deducted if you use the old matrix.
>
> 3. **Confusing dendrogram height with cluster size:** The **height** of a merge is the **distance** at which the merge occurred — *not* the number of points in the cluster.
>
> 4. **Not labeling linkage:** A numerical solution without stating "Single Linkage" / "Complete Linkage" loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 5. **Tie-breaking:** When two pairs have the same minimum distance, explicitly state your tie-breaking rule (usually the first pair encountered in row-major scan).
>
> 6. **Silhouette vs Dendrogram:** KTU sometimes asks for choosing $k$. Always mention **both** the **largest dendrogram gap** AND the **silhouette score** as valid methods.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Hierarchical clustering** produces a **dendrogram** — a full cluster hierarchy in one run.
- ✅ **Agglomerative (bottom-up)** is the standard; **divisive (top-down)** is exponential and rare.
- ✅ **Four linkage rules to memorize:** Single, Complete, Average, Ward.
- ✅ **Single** → chain-shaped; **Complete** → compact; **Average** → balanced; **Ward** → minimum SSE.
- ✅ The **Lance–Williams update formula** is the universal recursion — you do not need to recompute distances from scratch.
- ✅ **Ward's cost** is the **merge that minimizes within-cluster SSE increase** — derived as $\Delta = \frac{n_a n_b}{n_a+n_b}\Vert m_a - m_b \Vert^2$.
- ✅ **Number of clusters $k$** is chosen by cutting the dendrogram at the **largest vertical gap**.
- ✅ **Cophenetic Correlation Coefficient (CCC)** measures dendrogram quality — values near **+1** are best.
- ✅ **Complexity:** $O(n^3)$ time, $O(n^2)$ space — **not suitable** for very large datasets (use BIRCH or mini-batch K-Means instead).
- ✅ **Deterministic** — same input gives the same dendrogram every time (unlike K-Means).
- ✅ **Always state** the linkage rule and distance metric in your answer — KTU examiners deduct marks otherwise.
- ✅ Tie-breaking: prefer the **first minimum** encountered in row-major scan order.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: K-Means
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT751 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** K-Means

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of K-Means

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**K-Means Clustering** is a prototype-based, unsupervised, partitional clustering algorithm that partitions a dataset of **n** observations into **k** pre-specified, non-overlapping clusters, where each observation belongs to the cluster with the nearest centroid (mean). The algorithm operates on the principle of **iterative refinement**, alternating between two steps — *cluster assignment* and *centroid update* — until a convergence criterion is satisfied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMUT751 - Module 3):**
> K-Means is a centroid-based partitioning method that minimizes the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**, also called **inertia** or **distortion**, to produce compact, spherical clusters in the feature space.

Mathematically, K-Means solves the following optimization problem:

$$\min_{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^{2}$$

where:
- $C_i$ represents the $i^{\text{th}}$ cluster
- $\mu_i$ is the centroid (mean vector) of cluster $C_i$
- $\Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^{2}$ is the squared **Euclidean distance** between a data point $x$ and its assigned centroid

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **city planner** responsible for setting up **k = 3 fire stations** across a region to serve **n residential neighborhoods**. You want each neighborhood to reach the *nearest* fire station quickly, and each station to be located as close as possible to the homes it serves.

**The Process:**
1. **Initial Placement:** You randomly place 3 fire stations somewhere in the region (initial centroids).
2. **Assignment:** Every neighborhood is "assigned" to its nearest fire station based on straight-line distance.
3. **Relocation:** Each fire station *moves* to the **average location** of all neighborhoods currently assigned to it.
4. **Re-assignment:** Neighborhoods are again assigned to the nearest (now relocated) station. Some may switch stations.
5. **Repeat** until stations stop moving — this is convergence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy Summary:** K-Means is like a self-organizing postal system. Each delivery center (centroid) is repositioned to the geographic center of its customers, and customers are re-routed to their nearest center — this dance continues until no customer wants to switch centers.

### 1.3 Key Characteristics

| Characteristic | Description |
|---|---|
| **Learning Type** | Unsupervised (no labeled data required) |
| **Cluster Type** | Partitional (flat, non-hierarchical) |
| **Cluster Shape** | Tends to produce **spherical/globular** clusters |
| **Complexity** | $O(n \cdot k \cdot t \cdot d)$ where $t$ = iterations, $d$ = dimensions |
| **Sensitivity** | Highly sensitive to **initial seed positions** and **outliers** |
| **Scalability** | Scales well to large datasets (Mini-Batch K-Means variant available) |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Always mention that K-Means uses **Euclidean distance** by default unless specified otherwise. The algorithm is also referred to as **Lloyd's Algorithm** (proposed by Stuart Lloyd in 1957 at Bell Labs).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Algorithmic Decomposition — The "Why" and "How"

The K-Means algorithm is a textbook example of **Expectation-Maximization (EM)** style iterative refinement. Let us decompose its logic:

#### **Phase 1 — Initialization (Seeding)**

**Why?** The starting positions of centroids determine which local minimum the algorithm converges to. Random initialization can lead to poor clustering.

**How?** Choose **k** initial centroids using one of:
- **Forgy Method:** Randomly select **k** data points as initial centroids.
- **Random Partition Method:** Randomly assign each point to a cluster, then compute initial means.
- **K-Means++ (Arthur & Vassilvitskii, 2007):** Probabilistically spread initial centroids apart — *the recommended modern approach*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> K-Means++ initialization significantly reduces the probability of poor convergence and is **the default in scikit-learn**.

#### **Phase 2 — Cluster Assignment Step (Expectation Step)**

**Why?** Each data point must "belong" to exactly one cluster. We assign it to the closest centroid in feature space.

**How?** For each data point $x_j$, compute its distance to every centroid $\mu_i$ and assign it to the nearest one:

$$C^{(t)}(x_j) = \arg\min_{i \in \{1,\dots,k\}} \Vert x_j - \mu_i^{(t)} \Vert^{2}$$

#### **Phase 3 — Centroid Update Step (Maximization Step)**

**Why?** Once points are assigned, the centroid should move to the geometric center (mean) of its cluster to minimize squared distances.

**How?** Recompute each centroid as the arithmetic mean of all points currently assigned to it:

$$\mu_i^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_i^{(t)} \vert} \sum_{x_j \in C_i^{(t)}} x_j$$

where $\vert C_i^{(t)} \vert$ denotes the **cardinality** (number of points) in cluster $i$ at iteration $t$.

#### **Phase 4 — Convergence Check**

**Why?** Without a stopping criterion, the loop runs forever. We need a way to detect that the algorithm has stabilized.

**How?** Stop when any of the following is satisfied:
- Centroids no longer change: $\Vert \mu_i^{(t+1)} - \mu_i^{(t)} \Vert < \epsilon$
- Maximum iterations reached
- WCSS reduction between iterations falls below tolerance $\epsilon$

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective Function (WCSS)** | $J = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^{2}$ | Total within-cluster variance to minimize |
| **Euclidean Distance** | $d(x, \mu) = \sqrt{\sum_{d=1}^{D} (x_d - \mu_d)^{2}}$ | Default distance metric in K-Means |
| **Squared Euclidean Distance** | $d^{2}(x, \mu) = \sum_{d=1}^{D} (x_d - \mu_d)^{2}$ | Used inside WCSS for efficiency |
| **Centroid Update Rule** | $\mu_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum_{x \in C_i} x$ | New cluster center as mean of members |
| **Manhattan Distance (alt)** | $d(x, \mu) = \sum_{d=1}^{D} \vert x_d - \mu_d \vert$ | Alternative for high-dimensional sparse data |
| **Total Variance Decomposition** | $T = W + B$ where $W$ = WCSS, $B$ = BCSS | Fundamental Theorem of Clustering |
| **Elbow Method Criterion** | Plot $J$ vs $k$; choose elbow point | Selecting optimal **k** |
| **Silhouette Coefficient** | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | Validating cluster quality |
| **Algorithm Complexity** | $O(n \cdot k \cdot t \cdot d)$ | Time per iteration |
| **Initialization Methods** | Forgy / Random Partition / K-Means++ | First step of algorithm |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Note:** The relation **T = W + B** (Total variance = Within-cluster + Between-cluster sum of squares) is *frequently tested*. Maximizing $B$ is equivalent to minimizing $W$, which is exactly what K-Means does.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

K-Means is widely deployed across industries:

- **Customer Segmentation:** Marketing teams cluster customers by purchasing behavior.
- **Image Compression:** Reduce color palette from 16 million to **k** representative colors (vector quantization).
- **Document Clustering:** Group news articles by topic in NLP pipelines.
- **Anomaly Detection:** Points far from any centroid are flagged as outliers.
- **Sensor Network Partitioning:** Divide IoT devices into **k** energy-balanced zones.
- **Bioinformatics:** Gene expression clustering in microarray data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Manual K-Means Execution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a high-yield KTU Board Question Pattern (Dec 2022 / June 2023 type).** Students are given 8-10 2D points and asked to perform 2 iterations of K-Means with **k = 2**, then identify final clusters.

**Dataset (2D points):**

| Point | $x_1$ | $x_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| A | 2 | 10 |
| B | 2 | 5 |
| C | 8 | 4 |
| D | 5 | 8 |
| E | 7 | 5 |
| F | 6 | 4 |
| G | 1 | 2 |
| H | 4 | 9 |

**Initial Centroids (chosen by Forgy method):**
- $\mu_1^{(0)} = C_1 = (2, 10)$ *(point A)*
- $\mu_2^{(0)} = C_2 = (8, 4)$ *(point C)*

---

#### **ITERATION 1**

**Step 1.1 — Compute squared Euclidean distance from each point to both centroids:**

The squared Euclidean distance between point $P = (x_1, x_2)$ and centroid $\mu = (\mu_1, \mu_2)$ is:

$$d^{2}(P, \mu) = (x_1 - \mu_1)^{2} + (x_2 - \mu_2)^{2}$$

For Point A = (2, 10):

$$d^{2}(A, \mu_1) = (2-2)^{2} + (10-10)^{2} = 0$$

$$d^{2}(A, \mu_2) = (2-8)^{2} + (10-4)^{2} = 36 + 36 = 72$$

For Point B = (2, 5):

$$d^{2}(B, \mu_1) = (2-2)^{2} + (5-10)^{2} = 25$$

$$d^{2}(B, \mu_2) = (2-8)^{2} + (5-4)^{2} = 36 + 1 = 37$$

For Point C = (8, 4):

$$d^{2}(C, \mu_1) = (8-2)^{2} + (4-10)^{2} = 36 + 36 = 72$$

$$d^{2}(C, \mu_2) = (8-8)^{2} + (4-4)^{2} = 0$$

For Point D = (5, 8):

$$d^{2}(D, \mu_1) = (5-2)^{2} + (8-10)^{2} = 9 + 4 = 13$$

$$d^{2}(D, \mu_2) = (5-8)^{2} + (8-4)^{2} = 9 + 16 = 25$$

For Point E = (7, 5):

$$d^{2}(E, \mu_1) = (7-2)^{2} + (5-10)^{2} = 25 + 25 = 50$$

$$d^{2}(E, \mu_2) = (7-8)^{2} + (5-4)^{2} = 1 + 1 = 2$$

For Point F = (6, 4):

$$d^{2}(F, \mu_1) = (6-2)^{2} + (4-10)^{2} = 16 + 36 = 52$$

$$d^{2}(F, \mu_2) = (6-8)^{2} + (4-4)^{2} = 4 + 0 = 4$$

For Point G = (1, 2):

$$d^{2}(G, \mu_1) = (1-2)^{2} + (2-10)^{2} = 1 + 64 = 65$$

$$d^{2}(G, \mu_2) = (1-8)^{2} + (2-4)^{2} = 49 + 4 = 53$$

For Point H = (4, 9):

$$d^{2}(H, \mu_1) = (4-2)^{2} + (9-10)^{2} = 4 + 1 = 5$$

$$d^{2}(H, \mu_2) = (4-8)^{2} + (9-4)^{2} = 16 + 25 = 41$$

**Step 1.2 — Distance Matrix & Cluster Assignment (Iteration 1):**

| Point | $d^{2}$ to $\mu_1$ | $d^{2}$ to $\mu_2$ | Assigned Cluster |
|---|---|---|---|
| A(2,10) | **0** | 72 | **C1** |
| B(2,5) | 25 | **37** | **C2** |
| C(8,4) | 72 | **0** | **C2** |
| D(5,8) | **13** | 25 | **C1** |
| E(7,5) | 50 | **2** | **C2** |
| F(6,4) | 52 | **4** | **C2** |
| G(1,2) | 65 | **53** | **C2** |
| H(4,9) | **5** | 41 | **C1** |

**Cluster 1 (C1):** {A, D, H}
**Cluster 2 (C2):** {B, C, E, F, G}

**Step 1.3 — Recompute Centroids (Iteration 1 → 2):**

$$\mu_1^{(1)} = \left(\frac{2+5+4}{3}, \frac{10+8+9}{3}\right) = \left(\frac{11}{3}, \frac{27}{3}\right) = (3.67, 9.00)$$

$$\mu_2^{(1)} = \left(\frac{2+8+7+6+1}{5}, \frac{5+4+5+4+2}{5}\right) = \left(\frac{24}{5}, \frac{20}{5}\right) = (4.80, 4.00)$$

---

#### **ITERATION 2**

**Step 2.1 — Recompute distances using new centroids $\mu_1 = (3.67, 9.00)$ and $\mu_2 = (4.80, 4.00)$:**

For Point B = (2, 5):

$$d^{2}(B, \mu_1) = (2-3.67)^{2} + (5-9)^{2} = 2.79 + 16 = 18.79$$

$$d^{2}(B, \mu_2) = (2-4.80)^{2} + (5-4)^{2} = 7.84 + 1 = 8.84$$

For Point G = (1, 2):

$$d^{2}(G, \mu_1) = (1-3.67)^{2} + (2-9)^{2} = 7.13 + 49 = 56.13$$

$$d^{2}(G, \mu_2) = (1-4.80)^{2} + (2-4)^{2} = 14.44 + 4 = 18.44$$

**Step 2.2 — Updated Distance Matrix (Iteration 2):**

| Point | $d^{2}$ to $\mu_1$ | $d^{2}$ to $\mu_2$ | Assigned Cluster |
|---|---|---|---|
| A(2,10) | **2.79** | 51.84 | **C1** |
| B(2,5) | 18.79 | **8.84** | **C2** |
| C(8,4) | 27.09 | **10.24** | **C2** |
| D(5,8) | **2.79** | 16.04 | **C1** |
| E(7,5) | 20.19 | **5.96** | **C2** |
| F(6,4) | 11.65 | **1.44** | **C2** |
| G(1,2) | 56.13 | **18.44** | **C2** |
| H(4,9) | **0.11** | 26.44 | **C1** |

> The cluster assignments have **not changed** from Iteration 1. Therefore, the algorithm has **CONVERGED**.

**Final Result:**
- **Cluster 1:** {A(2,10), D(5,8), H(4,9)} → Centroid ≈ (3.67, 9.00)
- **Cluster 2:** {B(2,5), C(8,4), E(7,5), F(6,4), G(1,2)} → Centroid = (4.80, 4.00)

**Step 2.3 — Final WCSS Calculation:**

$$J = \sum_{x \in C_1} \Vert x - \mu_1 \Vert^{2} + \sum_{x \in C_2} \Vert x - \mu_2 \Vert^{2}$$

For Cluster 1:
$$J_1 = 2.79 + 2.79 + 0.11 = 5.69$$

For Cluster 2:
$$J_2 = 8.84 + 10.24 + 5.96 + 1.44 + 18.44 = 44.92$$

$$\boxed{J_{\text{total}} = 5.69 + 44.92 = 50.61}$$

### 3.2 Mathematical Proof of Optimal Centroid (Why Mean Minimizes WCSS)

**Claim:** Given a fixed cluster $C$ with points $\{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$, the value of $\mu$ that minimizes $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \Vert x_i - \mu \Vert^{2}$ is the **arithmetic mean** of the points.

**Proof:**

Define the objective function:

$$J(\mu) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Vert x_i - \mu \Vert^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \mu)^{2}$$

Take the derivative with respect to $\mu$ and set it to zero:

$$\frac{dJ}{d\mu} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} 2(x_i - \mu)(-1) = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \mu)$$

$$= -2 \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i - n\mu \right) = 0$$

Solving for $\mu$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i = n\mu$$

$$\mu = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$

Second derivative check:

$$\frac{d^{2}J}{d\mu^{2}} = 2n > 0$$

Since the second derivative is positive, $\mu$ is indeed a **minimum**. $\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithm Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START K-Means Algorithm] --> B[Input: Dataset X with n points and k value]
    B --> C[Initialize k centroids using Forgy or K-Means++]
    C --> D{Iteration Count Greater Than Max Iterations}
    D -->|No| E[Assignment Step: Compute distance from each point to all centroids]
    E --> F[Assign each point to the nearest centroid cluster]
    F --> G[Update Step: Recompute each centroid as the mean of its assigned points]
    G --> H{Convergence Check: Centroids Stable or WCSS Change Less Than Epsilon}
    H -->|Not Converged| I[Increment Iteration Counter]
    I --> D
    H -->|Converged| J[Output: Final Cluster Labels and Centroids]
    D -->|Yes Exceeded| J
    J --> K[END K-Means Algorithm]
```

### 4.2 Cluster Assignment vs Centroid Update Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase 1 INITIALIZATION] --> P2[Phase 2 EXPECTATION Assignment]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3 MAXIMIZATION Centroid Update]
    P3 --> P4{Converged}
    P4 -->|No| P2
    P4 -->|Yes| P5[Phase 4 OUTPUT Final Clusters]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of K-Means System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT BLOCK]
        I1[Raw Dataset X of n vectors in R to the d]
        I2[Cluster Count k parameter]
        I3[Max Iterations and Tolerance Epsilon]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS[CORE PROCESSING BLOCK]
        M1[Seeding Module: K-Means Plus Plus or Forgy]
        M2[Distance Computation Engine: Squared Euclidean]
        M3[Cluster Assignment Module: Arg Min Operator]
        M4[Centroid Update Module: Arithmetic Mean]
        M5[Convergence Monitor: Tolerance and Iteration Check]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT BLOCK]
        O1[Cluster Labels for each point]
        O2[Final Centroid Coordinates]
        O3[WCSS Inertia Score]
        O4[Cluster Membership Map]
    end

    I1 --> M1
    I2 --> M1
    I3 --> M5
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> M4
    M4 --> M5
    M5 --> M3
    M5 -->|Converged| O1
    M5 -->|Converged| O2
    M5 -->|Converged| O3
    M5 -->|Converged| O4
```

### 4.4 Cluster Quality Diagnostic Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D[Run K-Means for k Equals 1 to N] --> E[Compute WCSS for each k]
    E --> F{Elbow Detected}
    F -->|Yes| G[Optimal k is the elbow point]
    F -->|No| H[Compute Silhouette Score for each k]
    H --> I[Pick k that maximizes silhouette]
    I --> J[Validate Final Clustering using Silhouette Plot]
    G --> J
```

### 4.5 Limitations and Workarounds — Functional Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Limitation: Sensitive to Initial Seed] --> W1[Workaround: Use K-Means Plus Plus]
    L2[Limitation: Must Pre Specify k] --> W2[Workaround: Elbow Method or Silhouette Score]
    L3[Limitation: Assumes Spherical Clusters] --> W3[Workaround: Use DBSCAN or Spectral Clustering]
    L4[Limitation: Sensitive to Outliers] --> W4[Workaround: Use K Medoids or Remove Outliers]
    L5[Limitation: Local Minima Trap] --> W5[Workaround: Run Multiple n_init Times]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define K-Means clustering. State any two limitations of the K-Means algorithm.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 Marks):** K-Means is an unsupervised, partitional clustering algorithm that divides a dataset of **n** observations into **k** non-overlapping clusters by minimizing the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**. Each cluster is represented by its centroid (mean vector), and every point is assigned to the cluster with the nearest centroid.

> **Limitations (1.5 Marks):**
> 1. The user must **pre-specify the value of k**, which is often unknown in practice.
> 2. It is **highly sensitive to initial centroid selection**, often converging to local optima rather than the global optimum.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2023]**
*What is the role of the Elbow Method in K-Means clustering? How does it help in choosing the optimal number of clusters?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Role of Elbow Method (1.5 Marks):** The Elbow Method is a heuristic used to determine the optimal number of clusters **k** by plotting the **WCSS (inertia)** against various values of k. As k increases, WCSS decreases — but the marginal benefit diminishes.

> **Working (1.5 Marks):** The "elbow" point — where the rate of decrease sharply shifts — indicates the optimal k. Beyond this point, adding more clusters yields only marginal improvement in WCSS and risks **overfitting**. Thus, the elbow balances model simplicity against explanatory power.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): KTU Internal Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q.A. (a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure of the K-Means clustering algorithm. Discuss the role of initialization methods like K-Means++ in improving clustering quality. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Input Specification:**
The algorithm accepts three inputs:
1. A dataset $X = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ where each $x_j \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$.
2. The number of clusters **k**.
3. A convergence tolerance $\epsilon$ and maximum iteration count $T_{\max}$.

**Step 2 — Centroid Initialization (1.5 Marks):**
Initial centroids $\mu_1^{(0)}, \mu_2^{(0)}, \dots, \mu_k^{(0)}$ are selected. **K-Means++** improves this by:
- Picking the first centroid uniformly at random from the dataset.
- For each subsequent centroid, choosing a point $x_i$ with probability proportional to $D(x_i)^{2}$, where $D(x_i)$ is the distance from $x_i$ to the nearest already-chosen centroid.
- This **spreads centroids apart**, dramatically reducing the chances of poor local minima.

**Step 3 — Assignment Step (1.5 Marks):**
For each data point $x_j$, compute squared Euclidean distances to all centroids and assign to the closest:

$$C^{(t)}(x_j) = \arg\min_{i \in \{1,\dots,k\}} \Vert x_j - \mu_i^{(t)} \Vert^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Update Step (1.5 Marks):**
Recompute each centroid as the mean of points in its cluster:

$$\mu_i^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_i^{(t)} \vert} \sum_{x_j \in C_i^{(t)}} x_j$$

**Step 5 — Convergence (1 Mark):**
Stop if $\Vert \mu_i^{(t+1)} - \mu_i^{(t)} \Vert < \epsilon$ for all $i$, or if $t = T_{\max}$.

**Step 6 — Output (1 Mark):**
Return final cluster assignments and centroids.

**Why K-Means++ Works:** Empirical results (Arthur & Vassilvitskii, 2007) show that K-Means++ guarantees an $O(\log k)$-approximation to the optimal WCSS, compared to arbitrarily poor results from random seeding.

---

**Q.A. (b)** Given the 2D dataset below, perform **two complete iterations** of K-Means clustering with **k = 2**. Use the Forgy method to initialize centroids with points **P1(2,10)** and **P5(7,5)** as initial centroids. **(7 Marks)**

| Point | $x_1$ | $x_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 2 | 10 |
| P2 | 2 | 5 |
| P3 | 8 | 4 |
| P4 | 5 | 8 |
| P5 | 7 | 5 |
| P6 | 1 | 2 |

**Model Solution:**

**Initial Centroids:** $\mu_1^{(0)} = (2,10)$, $\mu_2^{(0)} = (7,5)$

**Iteration 1 — Distance Computations (2 Marks):**

$$d^{2}(P_1, \mu_1) = (2-2)^{2}+(10-10)^{2} = 0$$
$$d^{2}(P_1, \mu_2) = (2-7)^{2}+(10-5)^{2} = 25+25 = 50$$

$$d^{2}(P_2, \mu_1) = (2-2)^{2}+(5-10)^{2} = 25$$
$$d^{2}(P_2, \mu_2) = (2-7)^{2}+(5-5)^{2} = 25$$

$$d^{2}(P_3, \mu_1) = (8-2)^{2}+(4-10)^{2} = 36+36 = 72$$
$$d^{2}(P_3, \mu_2) = (8-7)^{2}+(4-5)^{2} = 1+1 = 2$$

$$d^{2}(P_4, \mu_1) = (5-2)^{2}+(8-10)^{2} = 9+4 = 13$$
$$d^{2}(P_4, \mu_2) = (5-7)^{2}+(8-5)^{2} = 4+9 = 13$$

$$d^{2}(P_5, \mu_1) = 50, \quad d^{2}(P_5, \mu_2) = 0$$

$$d^{2}(P_6, \mu_1) = (1-2)^{2}+(2-10)^{2} = 1+64 = 65$$
$$d^{2}(P_6, \mu_2) = (1-7)^{2}+(2-5)^{2} = 36+9 = 45$$

**Iteration 1 — Distance Matrix (1 Mark):**

| Point | $d^{2}$ to $\mu_1$ | $d^{2}$ to $\mu_2$ | Cluster |
|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | **0** | 50 | C1 |
| P2 | 25 | 25 | Tie* |
| P3 | 72 | **2** | C2 |
| P4 | **13** | 13 | Tie* |
| P5 | 50 | **0** | C2 |
| P6 | 65 | **45** | C2 |

*Ties are broken arbitrarily — assume P2 → C1, P4 → C1.*

**Iteration 1 — Cluster Assignments (1 Mark):**
- **C1 = {P1(2,10), P2(2,5), P4(5,8)}**
- **C2 = {P3(8,4), P5(7,5), P6(1,2)}**

**Iteration 1 — Centroid Updates (1 Mark):**

$$\mu_1^{(1)} = \left(\frac{2+2+5}{3}, \frac{10+5+8}{3}\right) = (3.00, 7.67)$$

$$\mu_2^{(1)} = \left(\frac{8+7+1}{3}, \frac{4+5+2}{3}\right) = (5.33, 3.67)$$

**Iteration 2 — New Distance Matrix (1 Mark):**

Using $\mu_1 = (3.00, 7.67)$ and $\mu_2 = (5.33, 3.67)$:

| Point | $d^{2}$ to $\mu_1$ | $d^{2}$ to $\mu_2$ | Cluster |
|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | **2.79** | 60.07 | C1 |
| P2 | **5.78** | 12.05 | C1 |
| P3 | 70.79 | **7.13** | C2 |
| P4 | **4.78** | 20.13 | C1 |
| P5 | 28.79 | **2.78** | C2 |
| P6 | 49.29 | **18.78** | C2 |

**Convergence Check:** Cluster assignments are stable. Algorithm has converged. (1 Mark)

**Final Output:**
- **Cluster 1:** {P1, P2, P4}, Centroid = (3.00, 7.67)
- **Cluster 2:** {P3, P5, P6}, Centroid = (5.33, 3.67)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): KTU Internal Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**Q.B. (a)** Explain the objective function of K-Means. Prove mathematically that the arithmetic mean minimizes the within-cluster sum of squared distances. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Objective Function (2 Marks):**
The K-Means objective function (also called **WCSS** or **inertia**) is:

$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^{2}$$

This represents the total squared Euclidean distance from every data point to its cluster centroid. The goal is to find cluster assignments $C_1, \dots, C_k$ and centroids $\mu_1, \dots, \mu_k$ that jointly minimize $J$.

**Mathematical Proof (5 Marks):**

*Consider a single cluster $C$ containing $n$ points $\{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ with $x_j \in \mathbb{R}$. We seek the value of $\mu$ that minimizes:*

$$J(\mu) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} (x_j - \mu)^{2}$$

*Step 1:* Differentiate with respect to $\mu$:

$$\frac{dJ}{d\mu} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} 2(x_j - \mu)(-1) = -2\sum_{j=1}^{n}(x_j - \mu)$$

*Step 2:* Set derivative equal to zero (first-order optimality condition):

$$-2\sum_{j=1}^{n}(x_j - \mu) = 0$$

*Step 3:* Expand and solve:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_j - n\mu = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_j$$

*Step 4:* Second derivative test:

$$\frac{d^{2}J}{d\mu^{2}} = 2n > 0$$

Since the second derivative is positive for $n \geq 1$, the critical point is a **minimum**. Hence, the arithmetic mean is the unique minimizer of WCSS. $\blacksquare$

> This proof generalizes trivially to $d$-dimensional vectors by applying the same logic component-wise.

---

**Q.B. (b)** Compare K-Means with K-Medoids. Discuss the strengths and weaknesses of each, and give a real-world scenario where K-Medoids is preferred over K-Means. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (3 Marks):**

| Feature | K-Means | K-Medoids |
|---|---|---|
| **Cluster Center** | Centroid = **mean** of points | Medoid = **actual data point** closest to mean |
| **Sensitivity to Outliers** | Highly sensitive (mean is distorted) | Robust (uses actual representative points) |
| **Distance Metric** | Only Euclidean (typically) | Any arbitrary distance (Manhattan, Cosine, etc.) |
| **Computational Cost** | $O(n \cdot k \cdot t \cdot d)$ | $O(k(n-k)^{2})$ per iteration — slower |
| **Cluster Shape** | Spherical, equal-sized | More flexible, non-spherical |
| **Algorithm** | Lloyd's algorithm | PAM (Partitioning Around Medoids) |

**Strengths of K-Means (1 Mark):**
- Faster, scalable to large datasets
- Simple to implement and interpret
- Produces tight, compact clusters when data is well-behaved

**Weaknesses of K-Means (1 Mark):**
- Cannot handle categorical features (mean undefined)
- Distorted by outliers
- Assumes convex, isotropic clusters

**Strengths of K-Medoids (1 Mark):**
- Robust to noise and outliers
- Works with any distance function
- Medoid is always an actual, interpretable data point

**Real-World Scenario Where K-Medoids Wins (1 Mark):**
In **medical diagnosis**, patient records contain outliers (rare diseases) and categorical attributes (blood type, gender). K-Medoids is preferred because:
- It resists distortion from outlier patients.
- It can use **mixed distance functions** (Hamming distance for categorical, Euclidean for numeric).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in K-Means Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to recompute centroids after assignment** — Marks lost if you stop after one assignment step.
> 2. **Using Euclidean (not squared Euclidean) distances** — While mathematically equivalent for assignment, K-Means implementations use **squared** distances for efficiency. Examiners expect squared distance tables.
> 3. **Not declaring convergence** — Always explicitly state "centroids unchanged → algorithm converged."
> 4. **Mixing up K-Means with K-Nearest Neighbors** — K-Means is **unsupervised clustering**; KNN is **supervised classification**.
> 5. **Ignoring the Elbow Method or Silhouette Score** — When asked about "choosing k," always mention **both** methods.
> 6. **Arithmetic errors in centroid computation** — Double-check mean calculations; this is where most marks slip.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — K-Means (PEMUT751, Module 3):**

- **Definition:** K-Means is an **unsupervised, partitional, centroid-based** clustering algorithm that minimizes **WCSS (Within-Cluster Sum of Squares)**.
- **Objective Function:** $J = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^{2}$
- **Two Key Steps:** (1) **Assignment** — assign each point to nearest centroid; (2) **Update** — recompute centroid as mean.
- **Convergence:** Stops when centroids stabilize, max iterations reached, or WCSS change $< \epsilon$.
- **Initialization:** K-Means++ is preferred over random seeding for better convergence quality.
- **Complexity:** $O(n \cdot k \cdot t \cdot d)$ — linear in dataset size, scales well.
- **Choosing k:** Use **Elbow Method** (visual WCSS plot) or **Silhouette Score** (quantitative measure).
- **Variance Decomposition Identity:** $T = W + B$ (Total = Within + Between cluster sum of squares).
- **Limitations:** Sensitive to initialization, outliers, requires pre-specified k, assumes spherical clusters.
- **Alternatives:** K-Medoids (robust to outliers), DBSCAN (arbitrary shapes), Hierarchical Clustering (no need to pre-specify k).
- **Evaluation Metrics:** Silhouette Coefficient, Davies-Bouldin Index, Inertia (WCSS).
- **Real-World Uses:** Customer segmentation, image compression, document clustering, anomaly detection, vector quantization.
- **Board Exam Mantra:** *"K-Means = Lloyd's Algorithm = Iterative Assignment + Centroid Update until convergence."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Big Data Analytics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT751 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Big Data Analytics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Big Data Analytics** is the systematic process of examining, cleaning, transforming, and modeling large, complex, and heterogeneous datasets (known as **Big Data**) to uncover hidden patterns, unknown correlations, market trends, customer preferences, and other useful business information, with the ultimate goal of supporting data-driven decision making.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Big Data Analytics is positioned as a **disciplinary intersection** of three core domains:
1. **Data Engineering** — storage, retrieval, and movement of massive datasets
2. **Data Science** — statistical inference, machine learning, and predictive modeling
3. **Business Intelligence (BI)** — translating analytical insights into strategic actions

The formal ISO/IEC 20547 reference model describes Big Data as *"data whose volume, velocity, or variety exceeds the capability of conventional relational database systems to capture, manage, and process within an acceptable elapsed time."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The City Traffic Control Room

Imagine the **Traffic Control Room of a Smart City** like Kochi or Bengaluru:

| Smart City Traffic Room | Big Data Analytics System |
|---|---|
| **Thousands of CCTV cameras** generating live feeds | Millions of IoT sensors, clickstreams, transactions |
| **Control Room Operators** who watch screens | Analytical engines (Spark, Hadoop) processing streams |
| **Pattern Recognition** (jam forming near Edappally) | Predictive models forecasting congestion |
| **Action** (rerouting signals green for north-bound) | Prescriptive analytics: dynamic pricing, recommendations |
| **Historical Records** stored in archives | Data Lake / Data Warehouse for retrospective analysis |

Just as the city cannot function with a single CCTV and a notebook — it needs a *control center*, *cameras*, *operators*, *algorithms*, and *historical logs* — Big Data Analytics requires an **ecosystem**, not just a database.

## 1.3 Why Big Data Analytics? — The Triggering Forces

> [!NOTE]
> The emergence of Big Data Analytics is the direct consequence of the **"Data Deluge"** — a 21st-century phenomenon where humanity generates more data in two days than was generated in the entire year 2003.

The three triggering forces are:
- **Exponential Data Growth** — driven by social media, mobile devices, IoT, and scientific instruments
- **Falling Storage Costs** — from \$1,000/GB in 1990 to less than \$0.03/GB in 2024
- **Maturation of Distributed Computing** — Google’s *MapReduce* (2004) and Apache *Hadoop* (2006) democratized parallel processing

## 1.4 Standard Industry Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value (Industry Benchmark) |
|---|---|
| Daily global data generation (2024) | **≈ 402.74 million terabytes** |
| IDC’s projected data sphere by 2025 | **175 zettabytes (ZB)** |
| Hadoop default block size | **128 MB** (HDFS standard) |
| 3 Vs baseline | **Volume, Velocity, Variety** |
| Extended Vs | **Veracity, Value** (IBM extension) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Foundational Framework — The 5 V's of Big Data

The **5 V's** is the universally accepted KTU 2024 Scheme framework for characterizing Big Data. Every Part A/B answer on this topic must invoke it.

### 2.1.1 **V₁ — Volume** (The Scale Axis)
- Refers to the **sheer size** of data, typically measured in **Terabytes (TB)**, **Petabytes (PB)**, **Exabytes (EB)**, and **Zettabytes (ZB)**.
- Sources: social media posts, sensor telemetry, transaction logs, scientific simulations.
- Engineering Implication: A single commodity server (with ≤ 1 TB RAM) cannot hold the data — **distributed storage is mandatory**.

### 2.1.2 **V₂ — Velocity** (The Speed Axis)
- Refers to the **rate at which data is generated, captured, and must be processed**.
- Categorized into four tiers:
  - **Batch** (e.g., nightly ETL jobs)
  - **Near-Real-Time** (e.g., minute-level dashboards)
  - **Real-Time** (e.g., fraud detection in milliseconds)
  - **Streaming** (continuous, unbounded — e.g., Twitter firehose)

### 2.1.3 **V₃ — Variety** (The Structure Axis)
- Refers to the **diversity of data types and sources**.
- Three structural categories:
  - **Structured** — relational tables, CSV, OLTP records
  - **Semi-Structured** — JSON, XML, log files, NoSQL documents
  - **Unstructured** — images, video, audio, free text, sensor waveforms

### 2.1.4 **V₄ — Veracity** (The Trust Axis)
- Refers to the **quality, accuracy, and trustworthiness** of data.
- Addresses noise, bias, missing values, contradictions, and provenance.
- **Real-world impact:** ≈ $3.1 trillion annual loss in the U.S. economy from poor data quality (IBM estimate).

### 2.1.5 **V₅ — Value** (The Utility Axis)
- Refers to the **business worth** extracted from the data.
- Data alone is *low-density* in value; it must be transformed via analytics to yield ROI.
- **Principle:** *"Data is the new oil — but only if refined."*

## 2.2 The Four Layers of Analytics (CRISP-DM Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme consistently tests the **4-tier analytical hierarchy**. Memorize this matrix — it carries **3 to 7 marks** in every ESE paper.

| Layer | Question Answered | Technique Used | Past Tense? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Descriptive Analytics** | *What happened?* | Dashboards, OLAP, Aggregation, Reporting | Yes (historical) |
| **Diagnostic Analytics** | *Why did it happen?* | Drill-down, Correlation, Root-cause analysis | Yes (retrospective) |
| **Predictive Analytics** | *What will happen?* | Regression, Classification, Time-series forecasting | Future |
| **Prescriptive Analytics** | *What should we do?* | Optimization, Reinforcement Learning, Simulation | Future + Action |

## 2.3 Big Data Analytics — Architecture Pillars

The **Lambda Architecture** (proposed by Nathan Marz, 2011) is the canonical reference:

$$
\Lambda_{\text{arch}} = \underbrace{B_{\text{atch\,Layer}}}_{\text{Master Dataset (HDFS)}} \;\oplus\; \underbrace{S_{\text{peed\,Layer}}}_{\text{Streaming (Storm/Spark)}} \;\oplus\; \underbrace{S_{\text{erving\,Layer}}}_{\text{Merged Queries (Druid/HBase)}}
$$

Where:
- **Batch Layer** — handles large-scale historical computation (MapReduce jobs)
- **Speed Layer** — handles low-latency real-time views
- **Serving Layer** — exposes merged queryable results to applications

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every quantitative and qualitative formula tested in KTU 2024 ESE for Big Data Analytics. Master this before attempting Part B.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Storage Conversion | $1\;\text{ZB} = 10^{21}\;\text{bytes}$ | Zettabyte | Bytes |
| 2 | Storage Conversion | $1\;\text{EB} = 10^{18}\;\text{bytes}$ | Exabyte | Bytes |
| 3 | MapReduce Speedup (Amdahl) | $S(n) = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{n}}$ | $p$=parallel fraction, $n$=nodes | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Hadoop Block | Default $B = 128\;\text{MB}$ | HDFS Block Size | MB |
| 5 | Cluster Capacity | $C_{total} = N_{nodes} \times D_{per\,node}$ | $N$=node count, $D$=disk/node | TB |
| 6 | Replication Factor | $R_{HDFS} \in \{1,\,2,\,3\}$ (default 3) | HDFS Replication | Integer |
| 7 | Logical Storage Used | $L = R_{HDFS} \times C_{logical}$ | Effective HDFS space | TB |
| 8 | CAP Theorem | Pick any 2 of $\{C, A, P\}$ | Consistency, Availability, Partition-tolerance | — |
| 9 | Streaming Throughput | $\lambda = \dfrac{E}{\Delta t}$ | Events per second | eps |
| 10 | Data Quality Index | $DQI = \dfrac{\#\,\text{Valid Records}}{\#\,\text{Total Records}} \times 100$ | Completeness | % |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sector | Big Data Analytics Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Healthcare** | Predictive readmission models, drug discovery via genomic data |
| **Banking & Finance** | Real-time fraud detection, credit scoring, algorithmic trading |
| **Smart Cities (Kerala context)** | Traffic signal optimization, water distribution analytics |
| **E-Commerce** | Recommendation engines (Amazon, Flipkart) |
| **Manufacturing** | Predictive maintenance on assembly lines (Industry 4.0) |
| **Agriculture** | Crop yield forecasting using satellite + soil sensor data |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Amdahl’s Law Applied to Hadoop Cluster Speedup

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):** A Big Data job has 80% of its execution time parallelizable across the cluster. If we scale the cluster from 1 to 10 nodes, what is the theoretical speedup?

**Given:**
- Parallel fraction: $p = 0.80$
- Serial fraction: $(1 - p) = 0.20$
- Initial nodes: $n_1 = 1$
- Scaled nodes: $n_2 = 10$

### Step 1 — State Amdahl’s Law
$$
S(n) = \frac{T(1)}{T(n)} = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \dfrac{p}{n}}
$$

**Logic:** The total execution time is decomposed into a *serial part* (which cannot be parallelized) and a *parallel part* (which scales with $n$). The serial part becomes the bottleneck at large $n$.

### Step 2 — Compute the Serial Component
$$
(1 - p) = 1 - 0.80 = 0.20
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Parallel Component
$$
\frac{p}{n} = \frac{0.80}{10} = 0.08
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Denominator
$$
(1 - p) + \frac{p}{n} = 0.20 + 0.08 = 0.28
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Speedup
$$
S(10) = \frac{1}{0.28} \approx 3.571
$$

### Step 6 — Interpret the Result
A **3.57× speedup** with 10 nodes is *far less* than the ideal 10× because 20% of the workload is locked into serial execution (e.g., driver program, metadata reads). This is a classic KTU examiner pitfall — students often quote $S \approx n$ without accounting for the serial fraction.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Amdahl’s Law assumes fixed problem size. For *scaling-data* problems (Big Data context), use **Gustafson’s Law**: $S_G(n) = n - p(n-1)$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hadoop Storage Footprint Calculation

**Problem:** A 5 TB dataset is ingested into an HDFS cluster with the default replication factor of 3. What is the *total physical storage consumed*?

**Given:**
- Logical data size: $C_{logical} = 5\;\text{TB}$
- HDFS replication factor: $R_{HDFS} = 3$

### Step 1 — Write the Storage Equation
$$
L = R_{HDFS} \times C_{logical}
$$

**Logic:** HDFS defaults to a *replication factor of 3* for fault-tolerance — every block is copied across three different DataNodes (two on the same rack, one on a different rack).

### Step 2 — Substitute Values
$$
L = 3 \times 5\;\text{TB}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute
$$
L = 15\;\text{TB}
$$

### Step 4 — Practical Note
In production clusters, this 3× overhead is offset by **Erasure Coding** (HDFS-EC, Hadoop 3.x), which reduces the overhead to ≈1.4× using Reed-Solomon codes — but this is a higher-order KTU question.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — The 4 V’s Applied to a Kerala State E-Governance Platform

> [!NOTE]
> This is the kind of **application-style 7-mark question** frequently asked in KTU ESE Module 4.

| V | Description in the E-Governance Context | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Volume** | The portal hosts records of **3.5 crore citizens** with ≈ 50 fields per record | ≈ 175 GB structured, plus petabytes of CCTV footage |
| **Velocity** | Aadhaar-linked authentication, online certificates | < 2 seconds response time SLA |
| **Variety** | Documents, biometric images, grievance audio clips | PDFs, JPGs, MP3, XML, JSON |
| **Veracity** | Inconsistent Aadhaar data, fake certificates, duplicate entries | Manual data entry errors, regional scripts |
| **Value** | Identifying subsidy-doubling, beneficiary tracking | Saves ≈ ₹2,000 crore annually via deduplication |

---

## 3.4 Algorithm Walkthrough — Big Data Analytics Pipeline (Conceptual)

The canonical KTU 2024 pipeline has **6 stages**. Every Part B 14-mark question expects you to draw and label this:

$$
\text{Raw Data} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Stage 1}}\; \text{Data Ingestion} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Stage 2}}\; \text{Data Storage} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Stage 3}}\; \text{Data Processing} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Stage 4}}\; \text{Data Analysis} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Stage 5}}\; \text{Data Visualization} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Stage 6}}\; \text{Decision}
$$

| Stage | Tools/Frameworks | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Ingestion | Apache Kafka, Flume, Sqoop | Streaming events into HDFS |
| 2. Storage | HDFS, HBase, Amazon S3, Cassandra | Distributed file system |
| 3. Processing | MapReduce, Apache Spark, Flink | Transformed datasets |
| 4. Analysis | Hive, Pig, Impala, Spark MLlib | Analytical models |
| 5. Visualization | Tableau, Power BI, Superset, D3.js | Dashboards |
| 6. Decision | Business rule engines, alerts | Actionable insights |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Big Data Analytics End-to-End Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    src1["Structured Sources RDBMS ERP CRM"]:::src
    src2["Semi Structured Sources JSON XML Logs"]:::src
    src3["Unstructured Sources Images Video Social"]:::src

    src1 --> ing1["Ingestion Layer Kafka Flume Sqoop"]:::ingest
    src2 --> ing1
    src3 --> ing1

    ing1 --> store1["Storage Layer HDFS HBase S3"]:::store

    store1 --> proc1["Batch Processing MapReduce Hive"]:::proc
    store1 --> proc2["Stream Processing Spark Flink Storm"]:::proc

    proc1 --> ana1["Analytical Layer Spark MLlib Mahout"]:::ana
    proc2 --> ana1

    ana1 --> viz1["Visualization Layer Tableau PowerBI Superset"]:::viz
    ana1 --> act1["Action Layer Alerts Recommendations Reports"]:::act

    viz1 --> dmn1["Decision Makers CEO CTO Analyst"]:::dmn
    act1 --> dmn1

    classDef src fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#000
    classDef ingest fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#f08c00,color:#000

```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — The 5 V's of Big Data (Concept Map)

```mermaid

  root(("Big Data 5 V's"))
    Volume
      Terabytes to Zettabytes
      Distributed Storage

    Velocity
```
